Ayahs

حمٓ (١)
Ha, Meem. (1)
تَنزِيلٌ مِّنَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ ٱلرَّحِيمِ (٢)
A [gradually] sent down [revelation] from the All-beneficent, the All-merciful, (2)
كِتَـٰبٌ فُصِّلَتْ ءَايَـٰتُهُۥ قُرْءَانًا عَرَبِيًّا لِّقَوْمٍ يَعْلَمُونَ (٣)
[this is] a Book whose signs have been elaborated for a people who have knowledge, an Arabic Quran, (3)
بَشِيرًا وَنَذِيرًا فَأَعْرَضَ أَكْثَرُهُمْ فَهُمْ لَا يَسْمَعُونَ (٤)
a bearer of good news and a warner. But most of them turn away [from it], [and] so they do not listen. (4)
وَقَالُوا۟ قُلُوبُنَا فِىٓ أَكِنَّةٍ مِّمَّا تَدْعُونَآ إِلَيْهِ وَفِىٓ ءَاذَانِنَا وَقْرٌ وَمِنۢ بَيْنِنَا وَبَيْنِكَ حِجَابٌ فَٱعْمَلْ إِنَّنَا عَـٰمِلُونَ (٥)
They say, ‘Our hearts are in veils [which shut them off] from what you invite us to, and there is a deafness in our ears, and there is a curtain between us and you. So act [as your faith requires]; we too are acting [according to our own].’ (5)

Content of Surah "Fussilat"

This Surah, by virtue of being a Meccan Surah, possesses the characteristics of Meccan Surahs, which emphasize Islamic knowledge, matters of belief, warnings, and glad tidings. At the same time, it addresses issues not raised in other Surahs of the Quran, which are among the unique features of this Surah.

Overall, the content of this Surah can be summarized into several sections:

  1. Attention to the Quran and numerous discussions surrounding it, appearing in various verses of this Surah. This includes the enduring sovereignty of the Quran and its logical dominance across all eras and ages, as mentioned in verses 41 and 42, which explicitly state: "Indeed, it is an unassailable Book. Falsehood cannot approach it from before it or from behind it." This also serves as proof of the non-distortion of the Quran throughout history, as well as the stubborn stances of enemies against this Heavenly Book, to the extent that they forbade people from listening to the verses of the Quran.

  2. Attention to the creation of the heavens and the earth, especially the beginning of the universe's creation from gaseous matter (dukhan) and the stages of the formation of the Earth, mountains, plants, and animals.

  3. References to the history of past arrogant and rebellious nations, including the people of 'Ad and Thamud, and their painful fate, along with a brief reference to the story of Moses عليه السلام.

  4. Warnings and threats to the polytheists and disbelievers, particularly through the mention of soul-shaking verses regarding the Day of Resurrection and the testimony of body parts—even the human skin—and the severe rebuke of the Lord toward them when they face Divine punishment.

  5. Some proofs of the Resurrection and the Day of Judgment, along with its characteristics and features.

  6. Various exhortations and counsels interspersed within the aforementioned topics, giving them more spirit and life, especially the call to steadfastness in the way of Truth, the method of logical interaction with enemies, and the manner of guiding them to the religion of Allah.

  7. Finally, the Surah concludes with an interesting and brief discussion regarding the "Signs in the horizons and within the souls" of the Lord and a return to the issue of the Hereafter.

The Virtue of Reciting This Surah

In a Hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, we read: "Whoever recites 'Ha Mim al-Sajdah,' Allah will grant him ten good deeds for every letter of it." [^1]

In another Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, it is stated: "Whoever recites 'Ha Mim al-Sajdah,' it will be a light for him on the Day of Resurrection as far as his eye can see, and a source of joy and happiness; and in this world, he will live a praiseworthy life, envied by others." [^2]

In another Hadith narrated by "Bayhaqi," Khalil ibn Murrah says: The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله would not go to sleep any night until he had recited Surah "al-Tabarak" and "Ha Mim al-Sajdah." [^3]

It is certain that the awakening verses of this Surah, with all those enlightening counsels and that rich, profound knowledge—provided they are absorbed into the human soul through recitation and become a guide for one's life—will be a light for the Resurrection and an effective means for victory in this world. This is because recitation is the prelude to reflection, and reflection is the prelude to action.

The naming of this Surah as "Fussilat" is taken from its third verse, and it is called "Ha Mim al-Sajdah" because it begins with "Ha Mim" and its 37th verse is a verse of prostration (Sajdah).

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ حم (1) تَنْزِيلٌ مِنَ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ (2) كِتابٌ فُصِّلَتْ آياتُهُ قُرْآناً عَرَبِيًّا لِقَوْمٍ يَعْلَمُونَ (3) بَشِيراً وَ نَذِيراً فَأَعْرَضَ أَكْثَرُهُمْ فَهُمْ لا يَسْمَعُونَ (4) وَ قالُوا قُلُوبُنا فِي أَكِنَّةٍ مِمَّا تَدْعُونا إِلَيْهِ وَ فِي آذانِنا وَقْرٌ وَ مِنْ بَيْنِنا وَ بَيْنِكَ حِجابٌ فَاعْمَلْ إِنَّنا عامِلُونَ (5)

Commentary

Once Again, the Grandeur of the Quran

It is mentioned in Islamic narrations that the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله constantly criticized the idols of the polytheists and recited the Quran to them so they might return to the path of Monotheism. However, they would say: "This is the poetry of Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله," and some would say: "This is soothsaying (kahanat)" (soothsaying consisted of predictions made by a group claiming connection with the Jinn), and others would say: "These are beautiful sermons that he recites (and has named 'the Quran')."

One day, Abu Jahl said to Walid ibn Mughirah—who was one of their famous figures, and whom the Arabs sought for judgment and opinion in their disputes and problems: "O Aba Abd al-Shams (Walid's kunya), what are these things that Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله says? Is it sorcery? Or soothsaying? Or a sermon?"

He replied: "Let me hear his words." He came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله while he was sitting in the Hijr Ismail and said: "O Muhammad! Recite some of your poetry to me!" He replied: "It is not poetry; rather, it is the Word of Allah with which He sent His Prophets and Messengers." He said: "Whatever it is, recite it!" The Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله began reciting Surah Ha Mim al-Sajdah. When he heard "بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ," he mocked it and said: "We have a man in Yamamah named 'Rahman'; it seems you are calling upon him!"

He replied: "No, I am calling upon Allah, who is the Compassionate (al-Rahman), the Merciful (al-Rahim)."

Then he continued. When he reached the verse: "But if they turn away, then say, 'I have warned you of a thunderbolt like the thunderbolt of ‘Ad and Thamud'" (Verse 13 of this Surah), Walid trembled upon hearing it, and his hair stood on end. He stood up, went to his house, and did not return to the Quraysh! The Quraysh said: "O Abu Jahl! It seems Walid ibn Mughirah has inclined toward the religion of Muhammad. Do you not see that he did not come back to us, accepted his words, and went to his home?" Because of this, the Quraysh became deeply saddened.

The next day, Abu Jahl went to him and said: "O Uncle! (Walid was Abu Jahl's uncle), you have made us hang our heads in shame and disgraced us!" Walid asked: "What has happened, my nephew?" He said: "Have you fallen in love with the religion of Muhammad?"

Walid said: "I have not become attached at all, and I remain upon the religion of my tribe and ancestors. However, I heard a powerful and complex speech from him that makes one's hair stand on end!" Abu Jahl asked: "Is it poetry?"

He said: "Absolutely not, it is not poetry."

He asked: "Is it rhythmic sermons?"

He said: "No, a sermon is a continuous and uniform speech, but these are varied words that do not follow the same meter, yet they possess a unique brilliance!" He asked: "Is it soothsaying?" He said: "No."

He asked: "Then what is it?"

He said: "Let me think about it!" The next day they asked: "O Walid! What did your thinking lead to?"

He said: "Say it is 'sorcery' because it captures hearts and carries them away!!" It was here that some verses of Surah al-Muddaththir (verses 11-30) were revealed concerning him. [^1]

This narration clearly shows to what extent the verses of this Surah are attractive and soul-shaking, such that they leave such a reaction even in a prejudiced Arab thinker.

Let us return to the commentary of the verses. Again, at the beginning of this Surah, we encounter the "Disconnected Letters" (al-Huruf al-Muqatta'ah)—"Ha Mim"—which appear for the second time at the start of Quranic Surahs. We have discussed the interpretation of the Disconnected Letters many times and see no need for repetition, except that some have considered "Ha Mim" to be the name of the Surah, or "Ha" to refer to "Hamid" (Praiseworthy) and "Mim" to refer to "Majid" (Glorious), which are two of the Great Names of Allah.

Then, referring to the grandeur of the Quran, He says: "This is a revelation from the Compassionate, the Merciful" (تَنْزِيلٌ مِنَ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ). The "General Mercy" of Allah and His "Specific Mercy" have joined hands to cause the revelation of these verses—verses that are a source of mercy for both friend and foe, and contain special blessings and mercies for the friends of Allah. In truth, the prominent attribute of this Heavenly Book, which is present throughout all its verses like a fragrance within the particles of a flower petal, is "Mercy"—mercy for those who follow its path and seek inspiration from its teachings.

After the aforementioned brief statement about the Quran, He proceeds to a detailed explanation, describing five attributes for this Heavenly Book—attributes that provide a clear and expressive depiction of the Quran's true face. First, He says: "A Book whose verses are explained in detail, and every matter is stated in its proper place, and the explanation and detail of all human needs in all fields are mentioned therein" (كِتابٌ فُصِّلَتْ آياتُهُ). [^1] "A Book that is eloquent and expressive" (قُرْآناً عَرَبِيًّا). "For a people who know and seek the truth" (لِقَوْمٍ يَعْلَمُونَ).

"A Quran that is a giver of glad tidings and a warner, both hope-giving and fear-inspiring; it gives glad tidings to the righteous and threatens the criminals" (بَشِيراً وَ نَذِيراً). "But most of them turned away, so they do not hear" (فَأَعْرَضَ أَكْثَرُهُمْ فَهُمْ لا يَسْمَعُونَ). [^2]

In this way, the first distinction of this great Heavenly Book is that various issues needed by humanity are clarified and legislated within it, such that anyone at any level of thought and at any stage of spiritual need can benefit from it according to their intellect and necessity.

Another description is that it is a complete collection, for "Quran" is derived from the root of qira'ah (reading), which originally means gathering the parts of speech together.

In the third description, its specific eloquence and clarity are stated, reflecting truths clearly and precisely, without deficiency or excess, expressively and eloquently, yet beautifully and attractively.

The fourth and fifth descriptions express its deep educational impact through glad tidings and warnings. Sometimes its verses soar so high in encouraging the good and the pure that they bring joy to one's entire being, and sometimes they are so soul-shaking in threatening and warning the corrupt and the criminals that one's hair stands on end. It carries these two educational principles side-by-side throughout its verses.

But alas, the stubborn prejudiced ones do not have a listening ear; it is as if they are deaf and hear nothing. Their physical ears are healthy, but they have lost the spirit of hearing and understanding the truths from the content of the speech.

However, the negative reaction of these blind-hearted ones did not end there; rather, they strove to make the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله despair of his mission and to prove to him that there is no listening ear for his call in this land, so do not strive in vain! As the next verse says: "And they said, 'Our hearts are within coverings from that to which you invite us, and in our ears is deafness, and between us and you is a partition!'" (وَ قالُوا قُلُوبُنا فِي أَكِنَّةٍ مِمَّا تَدْعُونا إِلَيْهِ وَ فِي آذانِنا وَقْرٌ وَ مِنْ بَيْنِنا وَ بَيْنِكَ حِجابٌ). "Now that it is so, do not concern yourself with us; you go about your work and programs, and we will act according to our beliefs and religion" (فَاعْمَلْ إِنَّنا عامِلُونَ).

Just like an ignorant and foolish patient who flees from a physician with a Christ-like breath and tries to use all means to separate himself from him.

First, they said: "Our intellect and thought are, as it were, placed in containers where nothing enters!" Note that "akinnah" is the plural of "kinan," meaning a covering—not just one covering, but in truth, coverings of ignorance and prejudice, coverings of stubbornness and enmity, coverings of blind imitation and the like had enveloped their hearts.

They said: "In addition to our 'intellect' not perceiving anything, our 'ears' are also heavy, and we do not hear your words"—meaning both the main center is out of order and the tools and instruments as well!

Beyond all this, it is as if a thick curtain has been drawn between us and you, such that even if we had healthy ears, your voice would not reach us. So why do you exhaust yourself so much, shouting, grieving, and preaching day and night? Leave us be, for your merchandise has no customers here! You follow your religion, and we follow our creed!!

This is the height of insolence, shamelessness, and ignorance—that a human being should flee from the Truth with their entire being in this manner: In the eyes of these black-hearted ones, the dawn is false, Even if someone performs the Miracle of the White Hand in the light!

It is noteworthy that they did not say: "wa baynana wa baynaka hijab" (and between us and you is a partition), but they added the word "min" to it and said: "وَ مِنْ بَيْنِنا وَ بَيْنِكَ حِجابٌ" to express greater emphasis. By adding this word, the concept of the sentence becomes: "The entire distance between us and you is filled by a partition." Obviously, a partition that covers this entire distance must be very thick, and naturally, speaking from behind such a partition will not have the slightest effect.

The phrase "فَاعْمَلْ إِنَّنا عامِلُونَ" (So work; indeed, we are working) might have been said by the disbelievers to make the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله despair, meaning: "You be busy with your program, and we are busy with the program of our own creed."

It is also possible that it is a type of threat toward the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, meaning: "Do whatever is in your power; we too will do whatever we can against your person and your religion." This expresses the ultimate extent of their stubbornness.

[^1]: and [^2]: Majma' al-Bayan, beginning of Surah Ha Mim al-Sajdah (Vol. 9, p. 2). [^3]: "Ruh al-Ma'ani," Vol. 24, p. 84. [^1]: "Bihar al-Anwar," Vol. 17, p. 211—This Hadith also appears with slight differences in other books, including Tafsir al-Qurtubi at the beginning of this Surah (Vol. 8, p. 5782).

Ayahs

قُلْ إِنَّمَآ أَنَا۠ بَشَرٌ مِّثْلُكُمْ يُوحَىٰٓ إِلَىَّ أَنَّمَآ إِلَـٰهُكُمْ إِلَـٰهٌ وَٰحِدٌ فَٱسْتَقِيمُوٓا۟ إِلَيْهِ وَٱسْتَغْفِرُوهُ ۗ وَوَيْلٌ لِّلْمُشْرِكِينَ (٦)
Say, ‘I am just a human like you. It has been revealed to me that your God is the One God. So worship Him single-mindedly and plead to Him for forgiveness.’ And woe to the polytheists (6)
ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْتُونَ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ وَهُم بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ هُمْ كَـٰفِرُونَ (٧)
—those who do not pay the zakat and disbelieve in the Hereafter. (7)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ لَهُمْ أَجْرٌ غَيْرُ مَمْنُونٍ (٨)
As for those who have faith and do righteous deeds, there will be an everlasting reward for them. (8)

Commentary

Who are the Polytheists?

These verses continue to speak about the polytheists and disbelievers, and in reality, they serve as a response to the statements quoted from them in the preceding verses, dispelling any illusion or misunderstanding regarding the mission of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

He says: "Say, I am only a human being like you, and this truth is constantly revealed to me that your deity is only one Deity" (قُلْ إِنَّما أَنَا بَشَرٌ مِثْلُكُمْ يُوحى‌ إِلَيَّ أَنَّما إِلهُكُمْ إِلهٌ واحِدٌ).

I do not claim to be an angel, nor a human from a superior race, nor am I God or the son of God; rather, I am a human like you, with the difference that the command of Monotheism (Tawhid) is constantly revealed to me. I never wish to compel you to accept this religion such that you would stubbornly stand against me and resist or threaten me, as you mentioned. I am placing a clear path before you, and I have no duty beyond this; the final decision rests with you.

Then He continues: "Now that it is so, turn all your attention toward this unique Deity and seek forgiveness and repent from polytheism and sin" (فَاسْتَقِيمُوا إِلَيْهِ وَ اسْتَغْفِرُوهُ‌) [^1]. Then, as a warning and a declaration of danger, He adds: "Woe to the polytheists" (و ويل للمشركين).

The next verse proceeds to define the polytheists, mentioning a characteristic in this regard that is unique to this verse. It says: "Those who do not pay the Zakat and are disbelievers in the Hereafter" (الَّذِينَ لا يُؤْتُونَ الزَّكاةَ وَ هُمْ بِالْآخِرَةِ هُمْ كافِرُونَ‌). In truth, they are identified by two things: the abandonment of Zakat and the denial of the Resurrection.

This verse has sparked much discussion among commentators, and many possibilities have been suggested for its interpretation. The primary reason is that Zakat is one of the branches of Islam; how can its abandonment be a proof of disbelief (Kufr) and polytheism (Shirk)?

Some have maintained the literal meaning of the verse, stating that abandoning Zakat—even if not accompanied by the denial of its obligation—is a sign of disbelief.

Others have considered abandonment accompanied by denial to be the proof of disbelief, because Zakat is among the essentials of Islam, and one who denies it is a disbeliever.

A group has said: Zakat here means purification and cleanliness, and the intended meaning of abandoning Zakat is failing to purify the heart from the filth of polytheism, just as it appears in Surah Al-Kahf, verse 81: "خَيْراً مِنْهُ زَكاةً" (a son purer than him).

However, the problem here is that the expression "لا يُؤْتُونَ" (they do not give or pay) is not compatible with this meaning.

Therefore, there is no way but to conclude that it refers to the payment of Zakat.

Another problem is that Zakat was legislated in Medina in the second year of the Hijra, while these verses are Meccan. In fact, according to some great commentators, Surah Fussilat is among the first surahs revealed in Mecca. Thus, they have been forced to interpret Zakat here as any form of "spending in the way of Allah," or to say that the principle of the obligation of Zakat was revealed in Mecca, but its limits, thresholds (Nisab), and specific amounts were revealed in the second year of the Hijra.

Tafsir Nemouneh; Vol. 20; p. 216

In any case, what is closer to the meaning of the verse here is that Zakat refers to the general concept of spending (Infaq), and its mention among the signs of polytheism is because financial spending in the way of Allah is one of the clearest signs of sacrifice, selflessness, and love for Allah, as wealth is among the most beloved things to man. Thus, spending or refusing to spend can serve as an indicator of polytheism or faith in many cases, to the extent that some love their wealth more than their own lives, and we have seen examples of this throughout life.

In other words, it refers to the abandonment of spending that indicates a lack of faith in God, which is why it is mentioned alongside the lack of faith in the Hereafter; or it refers to abandoning Zakat while denying its obligation. Another point that can help clarify the interpretation of the verse is that "Zakat" holds a special status among Islamic commands, and paying it was a sign of recognizing the Islamic government, while its abandonment was often considered a form of rebellion, defiance, and uprising against the Islamic government—and we know that uprising against the Islamic government leads to disbelief.

Evidence for this is found in Islamic history regarding the "Ahl al-Riddah" (the group that apostatized after the passing of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله). They were groups from the tribes of Bani Tayy, Ghatafan, and Bani Asad who refused to give Zakat to the officials of the Islamic government, thereby raising the flag of opposition. The Muslims loyal to the Quran fought them and crushed them.

It is true that at the time of this verse's revelation, the Islamic government had not yet been formed, but this verse can simultaneously be a subtle allusion to the aforementioned point.

It is recorded in history that the "Ahl al-Riddah" said after the passing of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "As for the prayer, we will pray; but as for the Zakat, our wealth shall not be usurped!" Following this event, the Muslims decided to fight this group and considered it proof of their apostasy. [^1]

In the last verse under discussion, the Quran introduces the group that stands in direct contrast to these stingy and faithless polytheists, and describes their reward, saying: "Indeed, those who believe and do righteous deeds shall have a reward that is eternal and never-ending" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ عَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ لَهُمْ أَجْرٌ غَيْرُ مَمْنُونٍ‌).

"Mamnun" is derived from the root "Mann," which here means "interruption" or "deficiency." Therefore, "Ghayru Mamnun" means "uninterrupted" and without deficiency. Some have also considered the word "Manun" (meaning death) to be from this same root, as well as "reminding someone of a favor verbally" (Mann), because the first is the interruption and end of life, and the second interrupts the blessing and gratitude. [^1] Some commentators have also said: "Ghayru Mamnun" here means that no favor will be held over the believers regarding this reward (but the first meaning seems more appropriate).

Key Point: The Extraordinary Importance of Zakat in Islam

The above verse, with its jarring expression, is a renewed emphasis on the importance of this Islamic obligation, whether it is taken to mean the obligatory Zakat or its broader and more general sense. And it must be so, because:

"Zakat" is one of the major factors for social justice, combating poverty and deprivation, closing class gaps, strengthening the financial foundation of the Islamic government, purifying the soul and spirit from the love of the world and money-worship, and in short, a very effective means for attaining proximity to God.

In many Islamic narrations, expressions have appeared showing that "abandoning Zakat" is on the verge of disbelief, similar to the expression used in the verses above. For example:

  1. In a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read that among the testaments of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to Ali عليه السلام was: "O Ali! Ten groups of this Ummah have disbelieved in Allah the Great," and he counted among them the one who withholds Zakat... Then he said: "O Ali! Whoever withholds a Qirat (a small amount) of the Zakat of his wealth is neither a believer nor a Muslim, and has no honor (before God). O Ali! The one who abandons Zakat will ask Allah to return to the world (to compensate for his great sin, but it will not be accepted), and that is the word of Allah, the Mighty and Sublime: 'Until, when death comes to one of them, he says: My Lord, send me back...'" [^1]

  2. In another Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, it is stated: "Indeed, Allah, the Mighty and Sublime, has ordained for the poor a portion in the wealth of the rich, for which they are not praised except by paying it, and that is Zakat; through it their blood is protected and by it they are named Muslims." [^2]

  3. Finally, in another Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "Whoever withholds a Qirat of Zakat, let him die if he wishes as a Jew or a Christian." [^1]

Regarding the importance of Zakat in Islam, its philosophy, the history of its obligation, and other related details, we have discussed them extensively in Volume 8, from page 6 onwards (under verse 60 of Surah At-Tawbah).

[^1]: (From the first page) The phrase "Fastaqimu" from the root "Istiqamah" here means to be straight toward something, and thus it is used with the preposition "ila" because it encompasses the concept of "Istiwa" (directing oneself). [^1]: (From the second page) "Tafsir Abu al-Futuh," Vol. 10, p. 9, under the verses discussed. [^1]: (From the third page) "Raghib" in Al-Mufradat under the root "Mann." [^1] & [^2]: (From the fourth page) Wasa'il al-Shi'a, Vol. 6, pp. 18-19 (Chapter on the establishment of disbelief, apostasy, and execution for withholding Zakat out of deeming it lawful or denial). A group of jurists and traditionists, such as the author of "Wasa'il," have interpreted the above narrations as referring to the denial of Zakat.

Ayahs

۞ قُلْ أَئِنَّكُمْ لَتَكْفُرُونَ بِٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ ٱلْأَرْضَ فِى يَوْمَيْنِ وَتَجْعَلُونَ لَهُۥٓ أَندَادًا ۚ ذَٰلِكَ رَبُّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٩)
Say, ‘Do you really disbelieve in Him who created the earth in two days, and ascribe partners to Him? That is the Lord of all the worlds!’ (9)
وَجَعَلَ فِيهَا رَوَٰسِىَ مِن فَوْقِهَا وَبَـٰرَكَ فِيهَا وَقَدَّرَ فِيهَآ أَقْوَٰتَهَا فِىٓ أَرْبَعَةِ أَيَّامٍ سَوَآءً لِّلسَّآئِلِينَ (١٠)
He set in it firm mountains, [rising] above it, blessed it, and ordained in it, in four days, [various] means of sustenance, equally for all seekers. (10)
ثُمَّ ٱسْتَوَىٰٓ إِلَى ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَهِىَ دُخَانٌ فَقَالَ لَهَا وَلِلْأَرْضِ ٱئْتِيَا طَوْعًا أَوْ كَرْهًا قَالَتَآ أَتَيْنَا طَآئِعِينَ (١١)
Then He turned to the heaven, and it was smoke, and He said to it and to the earth, ‘Come, willingly or unwillingly!’ They said, ‘We come obediently.’ (11)
فَقَضَىٰهُنَّ سَبْعَ سَمَـٰوَاتٍ فِى يَوْمَيْنِ وَأَوْحَىٰ فِى كُلِّ سَمَآءٍ أَمْرَهَا ۚ وَزَيَّنَّا ٱلسَّمَآءَ ٱلدُّنْيَا بِمَصَـٰبِيحَ وَحِفْظًا ۚ ذَٰلِكَ تَقْدِيرُ ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلْعَلِيمِ (١٢)
Then He set them up as seven heavens in two days, and revealed [to the angels] in each heaven its ordinance. We have adorned the lowest heaven with lamps, and guarded them. That is the ordaining of the All-mighty, the All-knowing. (12)

Commentary

The Eras of the Creation of the Heavens and the Earth

The above verses are examples of the "Signs on the Horizons" (Ayat al-Afaq) and the manifestations of Allah's greatness, knowledge, and power in the creation of the earth and the heavens and the beginning of the creation of beings. He commands the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to address the disbelievers and polytheists, asking them: Can they ever deny the God who is the Originator of these vast and expansive worlds? This is intended to awaken their conscience, intellect, and intelligence, calling them to judgment.

He says: "Say: Do you indeed disbelieve in Him who created the earth in two days?" (قُلْ أَ إِنَّكُمْ لَتَكْفُرُونَ بِالَّذِي خَلَقَ الْأَرْضَ فِي يَوْمَيْنِ).

"And do you set up equals and peers to Him?" (وَ تَجْعَلُونَ لَهُ أَنْداداً).

What a great mistake, and what a baseless claim!

"He is the Lord of the worlds" (ذلِكَ رَبُّ الْعالَمِينَ).

Is not He who currently governs this world the Creator of this heaven and earth? If He is the Creator and Governor, then how can you place these idols and fabricated deities alongside Him? Worthiness of worship belongs only to Him to whom the creation, governance, ownership, and sovereignty of the world belong.

In the next verse, addressing the creation of mountains, minerals, the blessings of the earth, and food sources, He says: "He placed therein firm mountains from above it, and He blessed it, and He ordained therein its various food supplies; all this was in four days" (وَ جَعَلَ فِيها رَواسِيَ مِنْ فَوْقِها وَ بارَكَ فِيها وَ قَدَّرَ فِيها أَقْواتَها فِي أَرْبَعَةِ أَيَّامٍ).

"These food supplies are exactly in proportion to the needs of the seekers and those who ask" (سَواءً لِلسَّائِلِينَ) [1]. In this way, Allah has anticipated the needs of all the needy and created what was necessary for all of them, and there is no deficiency or lack in it, just as He says in Surah Taha, verse 50: رَبُّنَا الَّذِي أَعْطى‌ كُلَّ شَيْ‌ءٍ خَلْقَهُ ثُمَّ هَدى‌:

"Our Lord is He who gave to each thing its form and then guided it."

The meaning of "seekers" (sa'ilin) here might be human beings, or it could be broader, including humans, animals, and plants (and if it is mentioned in the plural form for rational beings, it is by way of "predominance" or taghlib).

According to this interpretation, He anticipated from the beginning on earth not only the needs of humans but also the needs of animals and plants, and created what was necessary for the continuation of their lives.

[1]: Regarding the grammatical position (i'rab) of "sawa'an" and "lil-sa'ilin" and what they relate to, several possibilities have been suggested: First, that sawa'an is a circumstantial adverb (hal) for "food supplies" (aqwat) and lil-sa'ilin relates to sawa'an; the result would be the interpretation we provided above. Another possibility is that sawa'an is an adjective for "days" (ayyam), meaning these four periods were equal to one another, while lil-sa'ilin relates either to "ordained" (qaddara) or to an omitted word, implying: "existing for the seekers," meaning these four days are an answer for those who ask (but the first interpretation is the clearest of all).

Here an important question arises:

How is it that in the above verses, the creation of the earth is mentioned in two days, the mountains, blessings, and foods in four days, and following these verses, the creation of the heavens is also mentioned in two days, which totals eight days? This is while in numerous verses of the Holy Quran, the creation of the heavens and the earth is stated to be in six days, or in other words, in six eras [1]. Commentators have chosen two paths to answer this question:

The first path, which is famous and well-known, is that when He says "four days" (arba'ata ayyam), He means the completion of four days. Thus, in the first two days of these four, the earth was created, and in the next two days, the other characteristics of the earth were formed. Adding the creation of the heavens in two days, the total becomes six days (six eras).

Similar expressions exist in both Arabic and Persian; for example, it is said: "It takes ten days from here to Mecca, and fifteen days to Medina," meaning the distance between Mecca and Medina is five days, and the distance from here to Mecca is ten days [2]. Of course, if there weren't multiple verses stating creation in six days, such an interpretation would not be accepted; but since the verses of the Quran interpret one another and serve as context for each other, the above interpretation is well-accepted.

[1]: Refer to Surah Al-A'raf 54, Yunus 3, Hud 7, Al-Furqan 59, As-Sajdah 4, Qaf 38, and Al-Hadid 4.

[2]: According to this interpretation, the verse has an implied meaning like this: (and He ordained therein its food supplies in the completion of four days) or as stated in Tafsir al-Kashshaf: (all of that in four days).

Another path chosen by a small number of commentators is that:

"Four days" (arba'ata ayyam) does not refer to the beginning of creation but rather points to the four seasons of the year, which are the origin of the emergence of sustenance and the cultivation of food materials for humans and animals [1]. However, this interpretation, besides failing to maintain harmony among the phrases of the above verses—since in the case of the creation of the earth and heaven, "day" (yawm) means the era of the beginning of emergence, while according to this interpretation, "day" regarding the earth's characteristics and food materials would mean the seasons of the year which constantly repeat.

Furthermore, the result would be that out of the six days of creation, only two days related to the creation of the earth and two days related to the creation of the heavens are discussed, while the remaining two days, which relate to the creation of beings between the earth and heaven (ma baynahuma), are not mentioned.

In any case, the first interpretation seems more appropriate in several respects.

It perhaps needs no reminder that "day" in the above verses never means a conventional day, because before the creation of the earth and heaven, a day in this sense did not exist at all; rather, it refers to the eras of creation, which sometimes lasted millions or billions of years.

A full explanation of this meaning is provided in Volume 6 (under verse 54 of Surah Al-A'raf), page 200 onwards.

Two other points remain here that must be noted:

First, what is meant by "He blessed it" (baraka fiha)? It appears to refer to minerals, underground and surface resources, trees, rivers, and water sources which are the source of blessing and utility for all living beings on earth.

Regarding whether the expression "in four days" (fi arba'ata ayyam) relates to which of the subjects mentioned in the verse, some commentators have imagined it relates only to the issue of "food supplies" (aqwat), whereas this is not the case; rather, it relates to all three parts mentioned in the verse (the creation of mountains, the creation of the earth's resources and blessings, and the creation of food supplies), because otherwise, some of these matters would not fall within the days mentioned in the above verses, which would not be proportionate to the system of the verses.

After concluding the discussion regarding the creation of the earth and its evolutionary stages, He addresses the creation of the heavens, saying: "Then He turned to the heaven while it was smoke; then He said to it and to the earth: 'Come into being, willingly or unwillingly'" (ثُمَّ اسْتَوى‌ إِلَى السَّماءِ وَ هِيَ دُخانٌ فَقالَ لَها وَ لِلْأَرْضِ ائْتِيا طَوْعاً أَوْ كَرْهاً).

"They said: 'We come willingly and in obedience to the command'" (قالَتا أَتَيْنا طائِعِينَ).

"At this time, Allah created and completed them as seven heavens in two days" (فَقَضاهُنَّ سَبْعَ سَماواتٍ فِي يَوْمَيْنِ).

"And He ordained in each heaven its command," creating various beings and creatures in them and giving them order (وَ أَوْحى‌ فِي كُلِّ سَماءٍ أَمْرَها).

"And We adorned the nearest heaven with lamps of stars, and guarded it with meteors from the eavesdropping of devils" (وَ زَيَّنَّا السَّماءَ الدُّنْيا بِمَصابِيحَ وَ حِفْظاً).

Indeed, "That is the decree of the Almighty, the All-Knowing" (ذلِكَ تَقْدِيرُ الْعَزِيزِ الْعَلِيمِ).

In these two verses, there are important points that must be considered:

1- The expression "then" (thumma) usually denotes a delay in time, but sometimes it means a delay in mention (dhikr).

If it means the former, its concept is that the creation of the heavens took place after the creation of the earth and the creation of mountains, minerals, and food supplies. But if it means the latter, there is no obstacle to the creation of the heavens having occurred first and the earth afterward; however, at the time of explaining, He began first with the earth and its sustenance and resources, which are of interest and need to humans, and then proceeded to describe the creation of the heaven.

The second meaning, besides being more harmonious with scientific discoveries, also agrees with other verses of the Quran, as He says in Surah An-Nazi'at: أَ أَنْتُمْ أَشَدُّ خَلْقاً أَمِ السَّماءُ بَناها رَفَعَ سَمْكَها فَسَوَّاها وَ أَغْطَشَ لَيْلَها وَ أَخْرَجَ ضُحاها وَ الْأَرْضَ بَعْدَ ذلِكَ دَحاها أَخْرَجَ مِنْها ماءَها وَ مَرْعاها وَ الْجِبالَ أَرْساها مَتاعاً لَكُمْ وَ لِأَنْعامِكُمْ:

"Are you a more difficult creation or is the heaven? He constructed it. He raised its ceiling and proportioned it. And He darkened its night and brought out its brightness. And after that He spread the earth. He brought forth from it its water and its pasture, and the mountains He set firmly, as provision for you and your grazing livestock" (An-Nazi'at 27-33).

These verses clearly show that the spreading of the earth, the gushing of springs, and the emergence of trees and food supplies all took place after the creation of the heavens. Whereas, if we interpret thumma as a temporal delay, we would have to say all these occurred before the creation of the heaven; and since the phrase "after that" (ba'da dhalika) clearly counts all these as occurring after it, interpreting thumma as a delay in mention seems clear [1].

2- "Istawa" from the root istiwa' originally means balance or the equality of two things with each other. However, as some linguists and commentators have said, when this root is made transitive with "ala," it means "dominion and authority over something," such as: الرَّحْمنُ عَلَى الْعَرْشِ اسْتَوى‌ "The Most Merciful rose over the Throne [established His authority]" (Taha-5).

And when it is made transitive with "ila," it carries the meaning of "intending," as in the verse under discussion which says: ثُمَّ اسْتَوى‌ إِلَى السَّماءِ: "Then He intended the creation of the heaven."

3- The phrase "it was smoke" (hiya dukhan): "The heavens in the beginning were in the form of smoke" shows that the beginning of the creation of the heavens was from a vast and massive mass of gases, and this is completely harmonious with the latest scientific research regarding the beginning of creation.

Even now, many stars in the sky are in the form of a compressed mass of gases and smoke.

4- The phrase "He said to it and to the earth: 'Come into being... willingly or unwillingly'" (فَقالَ لَها وَ لِلْأَرْضِ ائْتِيا طَوْعاً أَوْ كَرْهاً) does not mean that words were actually spoken with sounds; rather, Allah's "saying" is that very existential command (al-farman al-takwini) and His will for the act of creation. The expression "willingly or unwillingly" points to the fact that Allah's definitive will had been attached to the shaping of the heavens and the earth, and in any case, those materials had to take such a desired form in the future, whether they "wanted" to or not.

5- The phrase "We come willingly" (atayna ta'i'in): "We took our final shape out of obedience" points to the fact that the constituent materials of the heaven and earth were completely submissive to Allah's will and command in terms of existence and creation. They accepted the necessary form and showed no resistance whatsoever to this divine command.

In any case, it is clear that that "command" and this "compliance" were not of a legislative or religious nature (tashri'i), but occurred purely from an existential (takwini) standpoint.

6- The phrase "He created them as seven heavens in two days" (فَقَضاهُنَّ سَبْعَ سَماواتٍ فِي يَوْمَيْنِ) refers to the existence of two eras in the creation of the heavens, each of which lasted millions or billions of years, and each era in turn is divided into other periods. These two eras might have been the era of the conversion of compressed gases into liquid and molten materials, and the era of the conversion of molten materials into solids.

We have said before: the use of "day" (yawm) and its equivalent in Persian (ruz) and other languages to mean "era" is very common and prevalent, and is even frequently seen in our everyday speech. For example, we say a person faces failure one day and becomes victorious another day, referring to the composition of life from different eras of defeat and victory.

An extensive explanation in this regard is mentioned in Volume 6, page 200 (under verse 54 of Surah Al-A'raf).

7- The number "seven" (sab') might be a "number of multiplicity" here, meaning We created numerous heavens and countless spheres. It might also be a "specific number," meaning the number of heavens is exactly seven, with the proviso that all the fixed stars and planets we see are, according to the testimony of the next phrase in this verse, part of the first heaven. Thus, the world of creation is formed of seven large sets, only one of which is positioned before human eyes; human scientific and research instruments have not penetrated beyond this region, i.e., beyond the first heaven.

But what are the other six worlds like? And what are they formed of? None knows but Allah.

This interpretation seems more correct (for more detail on this subject, study the commentary on verse 29 of Al-Baqarah in Volume 1, under the title "The Seven Heavens").

8- The phrase "And He ordained in each heaven its command" (وَ أَوْحى‌ فِي كُلِّ سَماءٍ أَمْرَها) points to the fact that the matter did not end merely with the creation of the heavens; rather, in each of them, He decreed specific beings, creatures, systems, and governance, each of which alone is a sign of His greatness, knowledge, and power.

9- The phrase "And We adorned the nearest heaven with lamps... and guarded it" (وَ زَيَّنَّا السَّماءَ الدُّنْيا بِمَصابِيحَ وَ حِفْظاً): "We adorned the lower heaven with the bright lamps of stars and created therein meteors that guard the expanse of the heaven from devils" is evidence that all stars are the adornment of the first heaven and appear to humans like lamps hanging from the ceiling of this azure sky. They are not only the adornment of the heaven—attracting the hearts of those who love the secrets of creation toward themselves with their special brilliance and meaningful, successive twinkling, singing the song of monotheism—but on dark nights, they are lamps for those lost in the deserts, guiding them with their light and determining the direction of movement.

"Meteors" (shuhub), which appear to our senses as fast-moving stars in the sky, are arrows that strike the hearts of devils and protect the expanse of the heaven from their infiltration (for an explanation of this subject, study Volume 11, page 40 onwards, under verse 17 of Surah Al-Hijr, and its supplementary explanation in Volume 19 under verse 7 of Surah As-Saffat).

10- The phrase "That is the decree of the Almighty, the All-Knowing" (ذلِكَ تَقْدِيرُ الْعَزِيزِ الْعَلِيمِ) is in fact a complement to the nine preceding phrases, and together they form a "perfect ten." It says: everything that has occurred in the heaven and earth from the beginning of creation, then the era of shaping and precise order, all had a calculated plan regulated by that Infinite Source of knowledge and power, and reflection and thought upon each one opens a path toward that Great Source.

[1]: What is narrated from Ibn Abbas, who says that the creation of the earth was prior but the "spreading of the earth" (dahw al-ard) was later, does not solve any problem; it is as if Ibn Abbas did not pay attention to the continuation of the verse which speaks of the creation of mountains and food supplies (observe carefully).

Ayahs

فَإِنْ أَعْرَضُوا۟ فَقُلْ أَنذَرْتُكُمْ صَـٰعِقَةً مِّثْلَ صَـٰعِقَةِ عَادٍ وَثَمُودَ (١٣)
But if they turn away, say, ‘I warn you of a thunderbolt, like the thunderbolt of ‘Ad and Thamud.’ (13)
إِذْ جَآءَتْهُمُ ٱلرُّسُلُ مِنۢ بَيْنِ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَمِنْ خَلْفِهِمْ أَلَّا تَعْبُدُوٓا۟ إِلَّا ٱللَّهَ ۖ قَالُوا۟ لَوْ شَآءَ رَبُّنَا لَأَنزَلَ مَلَـٰٓئِكَةً فَإِنَّا بِمَآ أُرْسِلْتُم بِهِۦ كَـٰفِرُونَ (١٤)
When the apostles came to them, before them and in their own time, saying, ‘Worship no one except Allah!’ They said, ‘Had our Lord wished, He would have sent down angels [to us]. We indeed disbelieve in what you have been sent with.’ (14)
فَأَمَّا عَادٌ فَٱسْتَكْبَرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ بِغَيْرِ ٱلْحَقِّ وَقَالُوا۟ مَنْ أَشَدُّ مِنَّا قُوَّةً ۖ أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَهُمْ هُوَ أَشَدُّ مِنْهُمْ قُوَّةً ۖ وَكَانُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا يَجْحَدُونَ (١٥)
As for [the people of] ‘Ad, they acted arrogantly in the earth unduly, and they said, ‘Who is more powerful than us?’ Did they not see that Allah, who created them, is more powerful than them? They used to impugn Our signs; (15)
فَأَرْسَلْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ رِيحًا صَرْصَرًا فِىٓ أَيَّامٍ نَّحِسَاتٍ لِّنُذِيقَهُمْ عَذَابَ ٱلْخِزْىِ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا ۖ وَلَعَذَابُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ أَخْزَىٰ ۖ وَهُمْ لَا يُنصَرُونَ (١٦)
so We unleashed upon them an icy gale during ill-fated days, in order that We might make them taste a humiliating punishment in the life of the world. Yet the punishment of the Hereafter will be surely more disgraceful, and they will not be helped. (16)

Commentary

Fear a Thunderbolt Like the Thunderbolt of ‘Ad and Thamud!

Following the impactful discourse on Monotheism and the recognition of Allah in the preceding verses, the verses under discussion sternly warn and caution the stubborn opponents who ignore all these clear signs and manifest verses. It says: "But if they turn away, then say: 'I have warned you of a thunderbolt like the thunderbolt of ‘Ad and Thamud'" (فَإِنْ أَعْرَضُوا فَقُلْ أَنْذَرْتُكُمْ صاعِقَةً مِثْلَ صاعِقَةِ عادٍ وَ ثَمُودَ).

Fear that those same deadly, fiery, and crushing thunderbolts might come for you and bring an end to your disgraceful lives. [^1] At the beginning of this Surah, we read that some of the leaders of the Meccan polytheists, such as "Walid ibn Mughirah" (and according to another narration, Utbah ibn Rabi'ah), came to the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) to investigate the Quran and his call, asking several questions. In his response, the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) recited the opening verses of this Surah to them. When he reached the above verses and warned them of a thunderbolt like that of ‘Ad and Thamud, they were so shaken and gripped by terror that they were unable to continue the conversation. They stood up, returned to their people, and expressed their profound distress caused by these anxiety-inducing words.

According to Raghib in Al-Mufradat, "Sa'iqah" (thunderbolt) is a terrifying sound produced in the atmosphere that is accompanied by fire, death, or punishment (which is why "Sa'iqah" is sometimes applied to death and sometimes to fire).

According to modern scientific research, a "Sa'iqah" is a massive spark of electricity created between a cloud carrying a positive charge and the earth carrying a negative charge. It usually strikes mountain peaks, trees, any elevated object, and in flat deserts, humans and livestock. Its heat is so intense that anything in its path is turned to ash, and it is accompanied by a terrifying sound and a severe earthquake at that point. We know that Allah punished several rebellious nations of the past using it, and remarkably, despite all the scientific progress achieved by mankind, there is no means to repel it, and man remains helpless in combating it.

But why, out of all nations, are ‘Ad and Thamud specifically pointed out here?

[^1]: The "Fa" in "Fa-in a'radhu" (But if they turn away), based on what has been said, is a "Fa of derivation" (Fa al-Tafri'), making this crushing warning a consequence of turning away from the preceding monotheistic verses.

This is because the Arabs were aware of their history and had seen the ruins of their cities with their own eyes. Furthermore, as a nomadic desert people, they were well aware of the dangers of thunderbolts.

Then it adds: "Remember when the Messengers came to them from every side—from before them and behind them—calling them to worship none but Allah" (إِذْ جاءَتْهُمُ الرُّسُلُ مِنْ بَيْنِ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَ مِنْ خَلْفِهِمْ أَلَّا تَعْبُدُوا إِلَّا اللَّهَ). The expression "from before them and behind them" may refer to what we mentioned above—that the Divine Messengers utilized every means of guidance and preaching, entering through every possible door to penetrate the hearts of these dark-hearted people.

It may also refer to the Messengers who came among these nations at different times, raising the call of Monotheism.

But let us see what response they gave to the immense and extensive efforts of these Divine Messengers! It says: "They said: 'If our Lord had willed, He would have sent down angels to convey His message to us, not humans like ourselves'" (قالُوا لَوْ شاءَ رَبُّنا لَأَنْزَلَ مَلائِكَةً).

Since this is the case, "We are certainly disbelievers in that with which you have been sent, and we do not consider these things to be from Allah at all" (فَإِنَّا بِما أُرْسِلْتُمْ بِهِ كافِرُونَ). The meaning of this statement is not that "you are indeed Messengers of God but we will not believe in your mission"; rather, it means "you have no mission at all; you are merely making a baseless claim, and for this reason, we will not submit to your words" (therefore, the phrase "that with which you have been sent" is either meant as mockery and sarcasm, or it refers to the mission they claimed to have).

This is the same excuse that the Quran repeatedly quotes from those who denied the call of the Prophets—that they expected a Divine Messenger to always be an angel, as if a human being is never worthy of this station. As stated in Surah Al-Furqan, verse 7: وَ قالُوا ما لِهذَا الرَّسُولِ يَأْكُلُ الطَّعامَ وَ يَمْشِي فِي الْأَسْواقِ لَوْ لا أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْهِ مَلَكٌ فَيَكُونَ مَعَهُ نَذِيراً: "And they say: 'What is this Messenger that he eats food and walks in the markets? Why was an angel not sent down to him to be a warner with him?'"

They were unaware that the leader of humans must be of the human species, so that he may be familiar with their pains, needs, problems, and various life issues, and thus be able to serve as their role model and example. Therefore, the Quran explicitly states in Surah Al-An'am, verse 8: "And if We had made him an angel, We would have certainly made him [in the form of] a man!" (وَ لَوْ جَعَلْناهُ مَلَكاً لَجَعَلْناهُ رَجُلًا).

In the following verses, as is the method of the Quran, after a brief mention, it goes into detail regarding ‘Ad and Thamud, saying: "As for ‘Ad, they were arrogant in the land without right (and all arrogance is without right), to the point that they said: 'Who is mightier than us in strength?'" (فَأَمَّا عادٌ فَاسْتَكْبَرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ بِغَيْرِ الْحَقِّ وَ قالُوا مَنْ أَشَدُّ مِنَّا قُوَّةً).

We know they were a people who lived in the land of "Ahqaf" in the region of "Hadramaut" in the south of the "Arabian Peninsula." They were unparalleled in terms of physical strength, financial capability, and material civilization. They built beautiful palaces and strong fortresses, especially erecting buildings on high places as signs of power and means of showing off. They were a harsh and warlike people, and this outward power had made them severely arrogant, such that they considered themselves an invincible population superior to everyone else. For this reason, they rose in rebellion, defiance, denial, and rejection against Allah and His Prophet, "Hud" (peace be upon him).

But the Quran, in response to this claim, says: "Did they not see that Allah, who created them, is mightier than them in strength?" (أَ وَ لَمْ يَرَوْا أَنَّ اللَّهَ الَّذِي خَلَقَهُمْ هُوَ أَشَدُّ مِنْهُمْ قُوَّةً).

Not only their Creator, but the Creator of all the heavens and the earth. These two powers are not comparable at all. Where is the insignificant, dependent, and fleeting power, and where is the infinite, eternal, and inherent power of the Truth? What relation does dust have with the Creator of the firmaments? Ma lil-turabi wa Rabbil-arbab? [^1] At the end of the verse, it adds: "And because of this baseless delusion, they used to constantly deny Our signs" (وَ كانُوا بِآياتِنا يَجْحَدُونَ).

Indeed, a shallow and small-minded person, when he feels a bit of power within himself, rises in rebellion, and sometimes even out of folly, rises to challenge the power of God. Yet, how simply and easily the Great Allah, with a single gesture, turns the factors of their life into the factor of their death. As He adds in the next verse regarding this story of ‘Ad: "So We sent upon them a screaming wind, loud, terrifying, cold, and fierce, in days of misfortune and dust, that We might make them taste the punishment of disgrace in the life of this world" (فأَرْسَلْنا عَلَيْهِمْ رِيحاً صَرْصَراً فِي أَيَّامٍ نَحِساتٍ لِنُذِيقَهُمْ عَذابَ الْخِزْيِ فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا).

This extraordinary gale, as mentioned in other verses of the Quran, would lift them from the ground and smash them down, like the trunks of date palms uprooted. [^1] This gale blew for seven nights and eight days, crushing the entire life of this tyrannical, selfish, and arrogant people, leaving nothing but ruins of those magnificent palaces, luxurious lives, and abundant wealth.

At the end of the verse, it says: this is only the punishment of this world, "and the punishment of the Hereafter is even more disgraceful" (وَ لَعَذابُ الْآخِرَةِ أَخْزى).

Such that all these painful punishments are like a spark compared to an ocean of fire! And hardest of all is that "no one will hasten to their aid, and they will not be helped from any side" (وَ هُمْ لا يُنْصَرُونَ).

Indeed, they spent a lifetime trying to show themselves as great; thus, Allah, at the time of punishment, ordained a humiliating penalty for them in this world and the next, to rub the noses of these arrogant transgressors in the dust.

"Sarsar" (on the pattern of daftar) is originally from the root "Sarr" (on the pattern of sharr), meaning to tie firmly. For this reason, a bag in which money was placed and tied firmly was called a "Surrah" (on the pattern of turrah). Later, it was applied to very cold winds, or loud winds, or poisonous and deadly winds. Perhaps the extraordinary gale that crushed the people of ‘Ad possessed all three of these characteristics.

"Ayyam Nahisat" means days of ill-omen and misfortune. Some have interpreted it as days full of dust or very cold days. A combination of these three meanings is also possible in the verses under discussion.

[^1]: In reality, this expression is similar to the phrase "Allahu Akbar," which introduces Allah as greater and higher than all beings, while in fact, the two are not comparable. However, because Allah speaks to us in our language, such expressions are found in His word.

[^1]: Surah Al-Qamar, verses 19-20, and Surah Al-Haqqah, verse 6 onwards.

Amir al-Mu'minin Ali (peace be upon him), as an awakening moral lesson in one of the sermons of Nahj al-Balagha, points to this very story of the people of ‘Ad, saying:

"Take warning in this world from those who said: 'Who is mightier than us in strength?' They were carried to their graves, though they were not called riders; they were lowered into the pits, though they were not called guests. Graves were made for them out of stone slabs, shrouds out of dust, and neighbors out of decayed bones!" [^1]

Key Points

1- What Was the Factor in the Destruction of the People of ‘Ad?

According to verse 13 of this Surah, both the people of ‘Ad and Thamud were destroyed by a "Sa'iqah" (thunderbolt), while the verses under discussion say they perished by a cold and fierce gale (Sarsar). Is there a contradiction between these two?

In response, it must be said that commentators and lexicographers have mentioned two meanings for "Sa'iqah": a general meaning and a specific one.

In the general sense, "Sa'iqah" refers to anything that destroys a person. As stated in Majma' al-Bayan: "The destroyer of everything."

The specific meaning is a massive fiery spark that descends from the sky and burns everything in its path, the details of which we explained in the commentary of these verses (this large spark results from the exchange of electricity between the cloud and the earth).

Therefore, if "Sa'iqah" is taken in the first sense, there is no contradiction with the gale.

Raghib says in Al-Mufradat: Some have said that "Sa'iqah" is of three types: "Sa'iqah" meaning death, meaning punishment, and meaning fire. Specifically, in the verse "I have warned you of a thunderbolt like the thunderbolt of ‘Ad and Thamud," it has been interpreted as punishment. Then Raghib adds: All these are gathered in one meaning: "Sa'iqah" is a loud sound that arises from the atmosphere, which sometimes contains only fire, sometimes another punishment, and sometimes death. The "Sa'iqah" is one thing, and these are its effects. [^1]

There is also the possibility that the people of ‘Ad were afflicted by two types of punishment: first, a crushing gale that smashed everything of theirs over a long period dominated their land, and then a deadly fiery thunderbolt seized them by Allah's command.

However, the first response is more appropriate considering other verses of the Quran that speak of the punishment of the people of ‘Ad. [^2]

2- The Ill-Omened Days of the People of ‘Ad

A group believes that the days of the year are of two kinds: ill-omened (unlucky) days and auspicious (blessed) days, and they have argued using the above verses. They say: there is a mysterious and unknown influence in days and nights whose effects we feel, but whose causes are obscure to us.

Meanwhile, others have interpreted "Ayyam Nahisat" in the verses under discussion as days full of dust.

The people of ‘Ad were caught in such a gale that they could not see one another, as can also be inferred from verse 24 of Surah Al-Ahqaf, which says: When the gale moved toward them, it was so dark and dusty that they thought a rain-bearing cloud was coming toward them, but they were told: "This is what you were seeking to hasten; it is a wind in which is a painful punishment."

By Allah's will, a more detailed discussion regarding "Auspicious and Ill-Omened Days" will follow under verse 19 of Surah Al-Qamar.

[^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 111. [^1]: Mufradat al-Raghib, root "Sa'aqa". [^2]: Refer to Surah Adh-Dhariyat verse 41, Surah Al-Haqqah verse 6, and Surah Al-Qamar verses 18 and 19.

Ayahs

وَأَمَّا ثَمُودُ فَهَدَيْنَـٰهُمْ فَٱسْتَحَبُّوا۟ ٱلْعَمَىٰ عَلَى ٱلْهُدَىٰ فَأَخَذَتْهُمْ صَـٰعِقَةُ ٱلْعَذَابِ ٱلْهُونِ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَكْسِبُونَ (١٧)
As for [the people of] Thamud, We guided them, but they preferred blindness to guidance. So the bolt of a humiliating punishment seized them because of what they used to earn. (17)
وَنَجَّيْنَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَكَانُوا۟ يَتَّقُونَ (١٨)
And We delivered those who had faith and were Godwary. (18)

Commentary

The Fate of the Rebellious People of Thamud

Following the explanation provided in the previous verses regarding the people of ‘Ad, the two verses under discussion speak of the people of Thamud, stating: "As for Thamud, We guided them (We sent the Prophet Salih with clear proofs to them), but they preferred blindness and misguidance over guidance!" (وَ أَمَّا ثَمُودُ فَهَدَيْناهُمْ فَاسْتَحَبُّوا الْعَمى‌ عَلَى الْهُدى‌).

"So the thunderbolt of a humiliating punishment seized them because of what they used to earn" (فَأَخَذَتْهُمْ صاعِقَةُ الْعَذابِ الْهُونِ بِما كانُوا يَكْسِبُونَ‌).

They were a group who lived in the land of "Wadi al-Qura" (a region between Medina and Sham). Allah had granted them flourishing, lush, and green lands and gardens full of bounties. They displayed great innovation and power in agriculture, possessed long lives, and had strong, powerful physiques. They were so skilled in constructing firm and advanced buildings that the Quran states in Surah Al-Hijr, verse 82: "They used to hew out houses from the mountains, [feeling] secure."

Their great Prophet came to them with powerful logic coupled with immense affection and accompanied by a divine miracle. However, this arrogant and self-satisfied people not only refused to accept his call but also caused great distress for him and his few companions. The result was that Allah afflicted these arrogant ones with a severe and humiliating punishment.

In Surah Al-A'raf, verse 78, we read that a violent earthquake seized them, and by morning, their lifeless bodies remained in their homes: "So the earthquake seized them, and they became [corpses] fallen prone in their homes" (فَأَخَذَتْهُمُ الرَّجْفَةُ فَأَصْبَحُوا فِي دارِهِمْ جاثِمِينَ‌).

In Surah Al-Haqqah, verse 5, it is mentioned that "the people of Thamud were destroyed by a devastating factor" (فَأَمَّا ثَمُودُ فَأُهْلِكُوا بِالطَّاغِيَةِ).

And in Surah Hud, verse 67, we read: "And the shriek seized those who had wronged, and they became [corpses] fallen prone in their homes" (وَ أَخَذَ الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا الصَّيْحَةُ فَأَصْبَحُوا فِي دِيارِهِمْ جاثِمِينَ‌).

However, in the verses under discussion, as we have seen, the term "thunderbolt" (sa'iqah) is used.

At first, it might be imagined that there is a contradiction between these expressions, but a little reflection shows that the four aforementioned terms [^1] refer back to a single reality:

For a "thunderbolt," as we have previously noted, possesses a terrifying sound which can be described as a heavenly "shriek" (sayhah); it also carries a scorching fire, produces a violent tremor (rajfah) at the point where it strikes, and is a devastating (taghiyah) instrument of destruction.

In truth, the eloquence of the Quran necessitates that the various dimensions of a single punishment be expressed through different terms in various verses to leave a deeper impact on human souls. In reality, they faced various lethal factors in a single event, each of which was sufficient on its own for their destruction: a "deadly shriek," a "lethal earthquake," a "scorching fire," and finally, a "terrifying thunderbolt."

However, since a group—albeit small—had believed in Salih عليه السلام, some might ask: What then was their fate amidst the terrifying wave of the thunderbolt? Did they also burn in the fire of the others? The Quran adds in the next verse: "And We saved those who believed and used to fear Allah" (وَ نَجَّيْنَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ كانُوا يَتَّقُونَ‌). It was the faith and piety of this group that saved them, while the disbelief and evil deeds of that rebellious group brought divine punishment upon them; each can serve as a model for groups within this Ummah.

Some commentators have said: Out of that entire population, only one hundred and ten people believed in Salih عليه السلام, and Allah preserved that small group and saved them in time.

Key Point: Types of Divine Guidance

We know that guidance is of two types: "Legislative Guidance" (al-hidayah al-tashri'iyyah), which is the "showing of the path" and pointing out the way with all its signs; and "Creative Guidance" (al-hidayah al-takwiniyyah), which is "attaining the objective" and leading one to the destination.

In the verses under discussion, both are gathered in one place. First, He says: "We guided the people of Thamud"—this guidance is the legislative guidance and the showing of the path. Then He adds: "They preferred blindness over guidance"—this refers to the creative guidance and reaching the destination [which they rejected].

Thus, guidance in the first sense, which is the definite duty of the Prophets of Allah, was achieved. However, guidance in the second sense, which depends on the will and choice of every human being, was negated by this arrogant and selfish people because they preferred misguidance over guidance (فَاسْتَحَبُّوا الْعَمى‌ عَلَى الْهُدى‌).

This is itself a clear and manifest proof of the issue of "man's free will" and the absence of compulsion in his actions. It is surprising that despite the explicitness and clarity of these verses, some commentators like Fakhr al-Razi, due to their preconceptions regarding the preference for the school of determinism (jabr), have insisted here on denying the verse's implication and have made statements that are far beneath the dignity of a researcher. [^1]

[^1]: "Rajfah" (earthquake), "Taghiyah" (overpowering blast), "Sayhah" (shriek), and "Sa'iqah" (thunderbolt). [^1]: Refer to "Tafsir al-Kabir" by Fakhr al-Razi under the verses discussed.

Ayahs

وَيَوْمَ يُحْشَرُ أَعْدَآءُ ٱللَّهِ إِلَى ٱلنَّارِ فَهُمْ يُوزَعُونَ (١٩)
The day the enemies of Allah are marched out toward the Fire, while they are in check, (19)
حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا مَا جَآءُوهَا شَهِدَ عَلَيْهِمْ سَمْعُهُمْ وَأَبْصَـٰرُهُمْ وَجُلُودُهُم بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (٢٠)
when they come to it, their hearing, their eyes and their skins will bear witness against them concerning what they used to do. (20)
وَقَالُوا۟ لِجُلُودِهِمْ لِمَ شَهِدتُّمْ عَلَيْنَا ۖ قَالُوٓا۟ أَنطَقَنَا ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنطَقَ كُلَّ شَىْءٍ وَهُوَ خَلَقَكُمْ أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ وَإِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ (٢١)
They will say to their skins, ‘Why did you bear witness against us?’ They will say, ‘We were given speech by Allah, who gave speech to all things. He created you the first time, and you are being brought back to Him. (21)
وَمَا كُنتُمْ تَسْتَتِرُونَ أَن يَشْهَدَ عَلَيْكُمْ سَمْعُكُمْ وَلَآ أَبْصَـٰرُكُمْ وَلَا جُلُودُكُمْ وَلَـٰكِن ظَنَنتُمْ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَعْلَمُ كَثِيرًا مِّمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ (٢٢)
You did not use to conceal yourselves [while perpetrating sinful acts] lest your hearing, your eyes, or your skins should bear witness against you, but you thought that Allah did not know most of what you did. (22)
وَذَٰلِكُمْ ظَنُّكُمُ ٱلَّذِى ظَنَنتُم بِرَبِّكُمْ أَرْدَىٰكُمْ فَأَصْبَحْتُم مِّنَ ٱلْخَـٰسِرِينَ (٢٣)
That misjudgement, which you entertained about your Lord ruined you. So you became losers.’ (23)

Commentary

In the preceding verses, the discussion pertained to the worldly punishment of the arrogant disbelievers and the criminal oppressors. However, the verses under discussion speak of their punishment in the Hereafter, enumerating the pains and calamities of the enemies of Allah across various stages of the Resurrection through several bone-chilling verses.

First, He says: "And [mention] the Day when the enemies of Allah will be gathered to the Fire" (وَ يَوْمَ يُحْشَرُ أَعْداءُ اللَّهِ إِلَى النَّارِ).

And so that their ranks remain joined together, "the front ranks are held back until the later ones catch up with them," and they are driven collectively toward Hell (فَهُمْ يُوزَعُونَ) [1].

[1]: "Yūza‘ūn" is derived from the root "waz‘" (on the pattern of wad‘), meaning to restrain or withhold. When this expression is used regarding an army or other ranks, its meaning is that the first of them are held back so that the last of them may join them.

"When they reach it, their ears, their eyes, and their skins will testify against them as to what they used to do!" (حَتَّى إِذا ما جاؤُها شَهِدَ عَلَيْهِمْ سَمْعُهمْ وَ أَبْصارُهُمْ وَ جُلُودُهُمْ بِما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ) [1]. What strange witnesses! They are members of the person's own body, and their testimony is in no way deniable, for they were present and observant in every scene, and they have been brought to speech by Divine command! Is the testimony of the limbs through Allah creating perception, consciousness, and the power of speech within them?

Or is it like the tree through which Allah created sound for Moses (peace be upon him)?

Or is it that the traces of sins, which were imprinted upon them throughout a lifetime, become exposed there on the "Day of Manifestation" (Yawm al-Burūz) and the day when hidden secrets are revealed? In common parlance, such traces are sometimes referred to as speaking or informing, as we say: "The color of the face gives news of the secret within"! All these interpretations are acceptable and have appeared more or less within the words of the commentators.

Of course, there is no obstacle to Allah creating perception and consciousness in them so that they testify out of knowledge and awareness in that great assembly; perhaps the apparent meaning of the verses at first glance suggests this as well. Regarding the glorification (tasbīḥ), praise, and prostration of the particles of the universe before Allah, a group holds the same belief.

[1]: "Mā" in the phrase "إِذا ما جاؤُها" is an augmentative mā (mā’ al-zā’idah) used for emphasis.

However, the latter meaning also does not seem very far-fetched, for we know that no entity in this world perishes, and the effects of our words and actions remain in our limbs and organs. Incidentally, this "existential testimony" (shahādah takwīniyyah) is the most eloquent and undeniable testimony, just as yellowness and pallor provide undeniable testimony of fear, and the reddening of the face testifies to anger or shame; applying the term "speech" to this meaning is perfectly acceptable.

But the second possibility—that Allah creates speech in them without them having perception or showing an existential effect—seems unlikely. In that case, it would be neither an instance of legislative testimony nor existential testimony; there would be neither intellect and consciousness in it, nor the natural effect of the action, and it would not hold the value of testimony in the presence of the Great Divine Court.

It is noteworthy that the phrase حَتَّى إِذا ما جاؤُها ("Until, when they reach it") indicates that the testimony of the members of the human body occurs at the court of Hell. Does this mean that such testimony takes place inside Hell, while Hell is the end of the matter? Or that their trial is held adjacent to Hell? The second possibility seems closer to the truth.

On the other hand: what is meant by "julūd" (skins - in the plural form)?

The apparent meaning is the skins of various parts of the body: the skin of the hands, feet, face, and others. If in some narrations it has been interpreted as "private parts," it is actually by way of mentioning an example (misdāq), not that the concept of julūd is restricted to that.

Thirdly, the question arises: why, among the body parts, are only the eyes, ears, and skins the witnesses of that court? Is the testimony restricted to these? Or do other members also testify?

What is understood from other verses of the Quran is that besides these, there are other witnesses from the body parts. In Surah Ya-Sin, verse 65, we read:

وَ تُكَلِّمُنا أَيْدِيهِمْ وَ تَشْهَدُ أَرْجُلُهُمْ بِما كانُوا يَكْسِبُونَ "Their hands will speak to Us, and their feet will testify to what they used to earn."

In Surah An-Nur, verse 24, the testimony of the "tongue," "hands," and "feet" is mentioned:

يَوْمَ تَشْهَدُ عَلَيْهِمْ أَلْسِنَتُهُمْ وَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَ أَرْجُلُهُمْ.

Therefore, it seems that other members will also testify, each in its turn. However, since most human actions are performed with the help of the eyes and ears, and the skins of the body are the first members to come into contact with actions, they are the witnesses in the front rank.

In any case, that day is the day of great scandal; a day when the entire existence of man begins to speak, revealing all his secrets, which plunges all sinners into a deep terror. It is here that "they turn to the skins of their bodies and cry out: 'Why did you testify against us?!'" (وَ قالُوا لِجُلُودِهِمْ لِمَ شَهِدْتُمْ عَلَيْنا).

"We who nurtured you for long years, protected you from cold and heat, washed, cleaned, and attended to you—why did you do this?!" "They will say in response: 'Allah, who has made everything speak, has made us speak'" (قالُوا أَنْطَقَنَا اللَّهُ الَّذِي أَنْطَقَ كُلَّ شَيْ‌ءٍ).

Allah has placed the mission of disclosure upon us on this day and in this great court, and we have no choice but to obey His command. Yes, the same One who created the power of speech in other speaking beings has placed this ability in us as well [1].

[1]: This interpretation holds if we translate the verse as: "Allah, who made every speaking thing speak, made us speak." However, there is also the possibility that "made everything speak" is absolute, meaning: "The God who has made all beings without exception speak—and today they reveal all secrets—has also made us speak. Do not be surprised by our speaking; all beings in the world are speaking today!"

Interestingly, they only ask this question of their skin, not of the other witnesses like the eyes and ears.

This might be because the testimony of the skin is the most wondrous and amazing, and the most extensive and vast. The very skin that must taste the Divine punishment before all other members rises to give such testimony, and this is truly astonishing.

Then they continue: "He is the One who created you the first time, and to Him you are all returned" (وَ هُوَ خَلَقَكُمْ أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ وَ إِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ).

And they further add: "If you used to hide your sins, it was not because you feared that your hearing, your sight, or your skins would testify against you; you did not believe at all that one day these would speak and testify against you" (وَ ما كُنْتُمْ تَسْتَتِرُونَ أَنْ يَشْهَدَ عَلَيْكُمْ سَمْعُكُمْ وَ لا أَبْصارُكُمْ وَ لا جُلُودُكُمْ).

"Rather, your concealment was because you thought that Allah did not know much of what you were doing" (وَ لكِنْ ظَنَنْتُمْ أَنَّ اللَّهَ لا يَعْلَمُ كَثِيراً مِمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ).

You were heedless of the fact that Allah is a Witness and Observer of your actions everywhere, aware of your inner and outer secrets, and His monitoring agents are with you everywhere. Can you ever perform an action hidden from your own eyes, ears, and even the skin of your body?!

Yes, you are so much within the grasp of His power and His hidden and manifest observers that even the tools of your sins are witnesses against you! A group of commentators has narrated a "reason for revelation" (sha’n al-nuzūl) for this verse: "Three individuals from the disbelievers of Quraysh and the tribe of Bani Thaqif, who had small skulls and large bellies, gathered beside the Kaaba. One of them said to the others: 'Do you think Allah hears our words?' Another added: 'Quietly! If we speak loudly He hears, and if we speak softly He does not.'

The third added: 'I think if He hears the loud voice, He surely hears the soft voice too!' It was then that the above verse was revealed" [1].

In any case, the next verse adds: "And that was your [ill] thought which you thought about your Lord; it has brought you to ruin, and you have become among the losers" (وَ ذلِكُمْ ظَنُّكُمُ الَّذِي ظَنَنْتُمْ بِرَبِّكُمْ أَرْداكُمْ فَأَصْبَحْتُمْ مِنَ الْخاسِرِينَ) [2] [3]. Are these dialogues the words of Allah—meaning the dialogue of the limbs and organs ends with the phrase "أَنْطَقَنَا اللَّهُ الَّذِي أَنْطَقَ كُلَّ شَيْ‌ءٍ," and then Allah's words begin regarding their deviation, misery, and ugly thoughts?

Or are these a continuation of the words of the skins of the body parts?

The second meaning seems more appropriate, and the expressions of the verse are more consistent with it, although the body parts also speak these words by Allah's command and His teaching, and the result of both is nearly the same.

[1]: This hadith has been narrated by many commentators with slight variations in wording, including Al-Qurtubi, Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, Fakhr al-Razi, Ruh al-Bayan, and Al-Maraghi. It also appears in Sahih Bukhari, Muslim, and Tirmidhi. What we quoted above was the translation of the version in Tafsir al-Qurtubi (Vol. 8, p. 95).

[2]: "Dhalikum" is the subject (mubtada’) and "zannukum" is its predicate (khabar). Some have also suggested that "zannukum" is an appositive (badal) and "ardākum" is the predicate of dhalikum.

[3]: "Ardākum" is from the root radā (on the pattern of ra’ā), meaning destruction or ruin.

Key Points

1. Good Expectations and Ill Expectations of Allah

The above verses clearly testify that an ill thought (sū’ al-zann) regarding Allah is so dangerous that it sometimes leads to the eternal destruction and punishment of man. An example of this was the thought of a group of disbelievers who imagined that Allah does not see their actions and does not hear their words; this very ill expectation became the cause of their loss and ruin.

Conversely, a good expectation (ḥusn al-zann) regarding Allah is a cause for salvation in this world and the Hereafter. As we read in a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام:

"It is fitting for a believer to fear Allah with such a fear as if he is overlooking the Fire, and to hope in Him with such a hope as if he is among the people of Paradise. Indeed, Allah the Almighty says: 'And that was your thought which you thought about your Lord...' Then he said: 'Allah is as His servant expects Him to be: if good, then good; and if evil, then evil.'" [1]

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse being discussed.

In another hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام from the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, it is stated: "The last person who is ordered to be taken to the Fire suddenly looks around. Allah the Great orders: 'Bring him back.' They bring him back. He is addressed: 'Why did you look around? And what command were you waiting for?' He replies: 'O Lord! I did not think this of You.' He asks: 'What did you think?' He replies: 'My thought was that You would forgive my sins and settle me in Your Paradise!' Allah says: 'O My angels! No, by My Might, My Majesty, My Favors, My Exaltation, and the Highth of My Station, this servant of Mine never thought well of Me for a single hour. Had he thought well of Me for an hour, I would not have bid him farewell with the Fire. Accept his lie and admit him into Paradise!' Then the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: 'There is no servant who thinks well of Allah the Almighty except that Allah will be as he expects Him to be, and this is what He says: (وَ ذلِكُمْ ظَنُّكُمُ الَّذِي ظَنَنْتُمْ...)'" [1]

2. Witnesses in the Court of Resurrection:

When we say that in the other world all humans will be tried, it might evoke images of courts like those of this world, where everyone appears before judges with a small or large file and witnesses like the witnesses of these courts, and a question-and-answer session takes place, and a final verdict is issued.

[1]: Tafsir of Ali ibn Ibrahim (as quoted in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 544).

But we have said many times that terms there take on a deeper meaning, the concepts of which are sometimes difficult, and occasionally impossible, for us prisoners of this world to imagine. However, whenever we pay attention to the indications mentioned in the verses of the Quran and the narrations of the Infallible Imams عليهم السلام, truths are revealed to us that partially unveil the greatness and depth of life in that world, showing what an extraordinary court the Resurrection is.

For example, when "the Scale of Deeds" (Mīzān al-‘Amal) is mentioned, the thought might arise that our deeds on that day will turn into light or heavy objects weighed in two-pan scales. But when we read in the narrations of the Infallibles that Ali عليه السلام is the Scale of Deeds—meaning the value of actions and the personality of individuals are measured against the existential standard of this great man of the world of humanity, and to whatever extent one resembles and is close to him, they have more weight, and to whatever extent they are dissimilar and distant, they have less weight—we realize what the Scale of Deeds means there! Regarding the issue of "witnesses," the verses of the Quran have also pulled back the curtain on truths, bringing in the testimony of things that are absolutely not considered in the courts of this world but play a fundamental role there.

Generally, it is understood from the verses of the Quran that there are six types of witnesses for that court:

  1. The most superior and highest of all is "the Pure Essence of Allah": "وَ ما تَكُونُ فِي شَأْنٍ وَ ما تَتْلُوا مِنْهُ مِنْ قُرْآنٍ وَ لا تَعْمَلُونَ مِنْ عَمَلٍ إِلَّا كُنَّا عَلَيْكُمْ شُهُوداً إِذْ تُفِيضُونَ فِيهِ": "And you are not [engaged] in any matter or recite any of the Quran and you do not do any deed except that We are witnesses over you when you are involved in it" (Yunus: 61). Of course, this testimony alone is sufficient for everything and everyone, but Allah's grace and His station of justice necessitated that He appoint other witnesses as well.

2. Prophets and Successors
