The Quran says: "فَكَيْفَ إِذا جِئْنا مِنْ كُلِّ أُمَّةٍ بِشَهِيدٍ وَ جِئْنا بِكَ عَلى‌ هؤُلاءِ شَهِيداً": "So how [will it be] when We bring from every nation a witness and we bring you [O Muhammad] against these [people] as a witness?" (An-Nisa: 41).

In a hadith regarding this verse in the book al-Kafi, we read from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام: "It was revealed specifically regarding the Ummah of Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله; in every century there will be an Imam from us among them, a witness over them, and Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله is a witness over all of us." [1]

3. The Tongue, Hands, Feet, Eyes, and Ears also testify

The Quran says: "يَوْمَ تَشْهَدُ عَلَيْهِمْ أَلْسِنَتُهُمْ وَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَ أَرْجُلُهُمْ بِما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ": "On the Day when their tongues, their hands, and their feet will bear witness against them as to what they used to do" (An-Nur: 24).

From the verses under discussion, it is also understood that the eyes and ears are among the witnesses, and from some narrations, it appears that all body parts in their turn testify to the actions they have performed. [2]

4. The Skins of the body also testify

The verses under discussion spoke explicitly about this subject, even adding that the sinners—who never expected the skins of their bodies to become witnesses against them—address them saying: "Why did you testify against us?" They reply: "Allah, who made everything speak, has made us speak" (Fussilat: 21).

[1]: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 1, p. 190. [2]: La'ali al-Akhbar, p. 462.

5. The Angels

The Quran says: "وَ جاءَتْ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ مَعَها سائِقٌ وَ شَهِيدٌ": "And every soul will come, with it a driver and a witness"—on that day every human enters the scene of gathering while an angel is with him driving him toward the reckoning, and a witness from the angels testifies to his deeds (Qaf: 21).

6. The Earth

Yes, the earth that is beneath our feet, and of which we are always guests, and which hosts us with its various blessings, is also precisely observing us, and on that day it will say all that is to be said, as we read: "يَوْمَئِذٍ تُحَدِّثُ أَخْبارَها": "That Day, it will report its news" (Az-Zalzalah: 4).

7. Time is also among the witnesses

Although this is not mentioned in the text of the Quranic verses, there is evidence for it in the narrations of the Infallibles عليهم السلام, as we read from Ali عليه السلام:

"No day passes for the son of Adam except that that day says to him: 'O son of Adam! I am a new day, and I am a witness over you. So speak good in me and do good in me, that I may testify in your favor on the Day of Resurrection.'" [1] Truly, it is amazing—all these witnesses of truth and the observers of that great court, from time and place to the angels, our body parts, the prophets, the saints, and above all, the Pure Essence of Allah, are monitoring our actions and are witnesses over us, and how heedless we are! Is not belief in the existence of such monitors enough to place a person completely on the path of truth, justice, purity, and piety?

[1]: Safinat al-Bihar, Vol. 2, root yawm.

Ayahs

فَإِن يَصْبِرُوا۟ فَٱلنَّارُ مَثْوًى لَّهُمْ ۖ وَإِن يَسْتَعْتِبُوا۟ فَمَا هُم مِّنَ ٱلْمُعْتَبِينَ (٢٤)
Should they be patient, the Fire is their abode; and should they seek to propitiate [Allah], they will not be redeemed. (24)
۞ وَقَيَّضْنَا لَهُمْ قُرَنَآءَ فَزَيَّنُوا۟ لَهُم مَّا بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَمَا خَلْفَهُمْ وَحَقَّ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْقَوْلُ فِىٓ أُمَمٍ قَدْ خَلَتْ مِن قَبْلِهِم مِّنَ ٱلْجِنِّ وَٱلْإِنسِ ۖ إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ خَـٰسِرِينَ (٢٥)
We have assigned them companions who make their present [conduct] and future [state] seem decorous to them, and the word became due against them along with the nations of jinn and humans that passed away before them. They were indeed losers. (25)

Commentary

Evil Companions

Following the discussion in the previous verses regarding the fate of the enemies of Allah (اعداء الله), the two verses under discussion point to two parts of their painful punishment in the Hereafter and in this world.

First, Allah says: "Whether they endure with patience or not, the Fire of Hell is their abode," and escape from it is impossible (فَإِنْ يَصْبِرُوا فَالنَّارُ مَثْوىً لَهُمْ) [^1]. "Mathwa" is derived from the root "thawa" (rhyming with hawa), meaning a dwelling place or a place of settlement.

This verse is, in fact, similar to verse 16 of Surah At-Tur, which says: اصْلَوْها فَاصْبِرُوا أَوْ لا تَصْبِرُوا سَواءٌ عَلَيْكُمْ: "Enter the Fire of Hell; whether you are patient or impatient, it is all the same for you." It is also like verse 21 of Surah Ibrahim: سَواءٌ عَلَيْنا أَ جَزِعْنا أَمْ صَبَرْنا ما لَنا مِنْ مَحِيصٍ: "It is all the same to us whether we show intolerance or remain patient; there is for us no place of escape."

Then, to emphasize this point, He adds: "And if they seek to please [Allah] and ask for His pardon, it will lead nowhere and they will not be among those allowed to please [Him]" (وَ إِنْ يَسْتَعْتِبُوا فَما هُمْ مِنَ الْمُعْتَبِينَ).

"Yasta'tibu" is originally derived from "atab," which means to express harshness. Its concept is that the sinful person submits himself to the reproaches of the one whose rights were violated so that he may be forgiven and satisfied. Therefore, this root (isti'tab) is also used to mean seeking pleasure and requesting pardon [^2].

Then, He refers to their painful worldly punishment, saying: "We assigned for them companions and associates of evil thoughts and wicked nature, who made everything seem fair-seeming to them," presenting ugliness as beauty and evils as virtues (وَ قَيَّضْنا لَهُمْ قُرَناءَ فَزَيَّنُوا لَهُمْ ما بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَ ما خَلْفَهُمْ).

"Qayyadna" is from the root "qayd" (rhyming with fayd), which originally means the shell of an egg. Subsequently, it is used in cases where individuals gain complete mastery over a person, similar to the mastery of the shell over the egg. This indicates that these wicked and corrupt friends surround them from every side, steal their thoughts, and dominate them to such an extent that they lose their sense of discernment, and ugliness appears beautiful to them. How painful such a state is for a human being, for he easily sinks into the whirlpool of corruption, and the doors of salvation are closed to him.

Sometimes the root "qayyadna" is also used in the sense of replacing one thing with another. According to this meaning, the interpretation of the verse would be that We take away righteous friends from them and give them corrupt friends instead.

This same meaning is expressed more vividly in verses 36 and 37 of Surah Az-Zukhruf:

وَ مَنْ يَعْشُ عَنْ ذِكْرِ الرَّحْمنِ نُقَيِّضْ لَهُ شَيْطاناً فَهو لَهُ قَرِينٌ وَ إِنَّهُمْ لَيَصُدُّونَهُمْ عَنِ السَّبِيلِ وَ يَحْسَبُونَ أَنَّهُمْ مُهْتَدُونَ: "And whoever turns away from the remembrance of the Most Merciful—We appoint for him a devil, and he is to him a companion. And indeed, they avert them from the way [of guidance] while they think that they are [rightly] guided."

Truly, when we look at the gatherings of oppressors, corrupters, and criminals, we clearly see the footprints of these devils in their lives—seductive associates who surround them from every side, dominate their brains and thoughts, and present realities to them in a distorted manner.

The phrase "what is before them and what is behind them" (ما بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَ ما خَلْفَهُمْ) may refer to the all-encompassing surrounding of these devils and their beautification [of sin].

It has also been suggested that "what is before them" refers to the pleasures and glitters of this world, and "what is behind them" refers to the denial of the Resurrection and the Day of Judgment.

Another possible interpretation is that "what is before them" refers to their current worldly situation, and "what is behind them" refers to the future that lies ahead for them and their children, as many crimes are usually committed for the sake of securing their future.

Then He adds: "Because of this deplorable situation, the decree of Allah's punishment was fulfilled against them, and they met the fate of the lost nations of jinn and men who preceded them" (وَ حَقَّ عَلَيْهِمُ الْقَوْلُ فِي أُمَمٍ قَدْ خَلَتْ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ مِنَ الْجِنِّ وَ الْإِنْسِ) [^1]. Then He ends the verse with this sentence: "Indeed, they were losers" (إِنَّهُمْ كانُوا خاسِرِينَ).

These expressions are, in fact, the opposite of the expressions we read in the subsequent verses regarding the steadfast believers, whose companions in this world and the Hereafter are the angels, who give them the glad tidings that there shall be no fear or grief for them.

[^1]: The verse is implied as: "Whether they are patient or not patient, the Fire is their abode." [^2]: "Mufradat al-Raghib" and "Lisan al-Arab" under the root "ataba." [^1]: "Fi umamin" (among nations) relates to a hidden verb, implied as: "being among nations that have passed..." It has also been suggested that "fi" here means "ma'a" (with).

Ayahs

وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لَا تَسْمَعُوا۟ لِهَـٰذَا ٱلْقُرْءَانِ وَٱلْغَوْا۟ فِيهِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَغْلِبُونَ (٢٦)
The faithless say, ‘Do not listen to this Quran and hoot it down so that you may prevail [over the Apostle].’ (26)
فَلَنُذِيقَنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ عَذَابًا شَدِيدًا وَلَنَجْزِيَنَّهُمْ أَسْوَأَ ٱلَّذِى كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (٢٧)
We will surely make the faithless taste a severe punishment, and We will surely requite them by the worst of what they used to do. (27)
ذَٰلِكَ جَزَآءُ أَعْدَآءِ ٱللَّهِ ٱلنَّارُ ۖ لَهُمْ فِيهَا دَارُ ٱلْخُلْدِ ۖ جَزَآءًۢ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا يَجْحَدُونَ (٢٨)
That is the requital of the enemies of Allah—the Fire! In it, they will have an everlasting abode, as a requital for their impugning Our signs. (28)
وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ رَبَّنَآ أَرِنَا ٱلَّذَيْنِ أَضَلَّانَا مِنَ ٱلْجِنِّ وَٱلْإِنسِ نَجْعَلْهُمَا تَحْتَ أَقْدَامِنَا لِيَكُونَا مِنَ ٱلْأَسْفَلِينَ (٢٩)
The faithless will say, ‘Our Lord! Show us those who led us astray from among jinn and humans so that we may trample them under our feet, so that they may be among the lowermost!’ (29)

Commentary

Create a Tumult So They May Not Hear the Melodious Voice of the Quran!

In connection with the discussions regarding some previous nations, the people of ‘Ad and Thamud, in the preceding verses, and also in connection with the evil-natured companions who portray realities as inverted in the eyes of man, the verses under discussion depict a corner of the deviation and ill-thinking of the polytheists during the era of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله).

It is mentioned in some narrations that whenever the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) raised his voice in Mecca to recite the Holy Quran and the sweet, attractive, and profound words of Allah, the polytheists would drive people away from him, saying: "Whistle and shout, and raise your voices with poetry so that his words may not be heard!" [^1] The Holy Quran refers to this meaning in the above verses, saying: "The disbelievers said: 'Do not listen to this Quran, and make noise and create a tumult during its recitation, so that you may prevail!'" (وَ قالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لا تَسْمَعُوا لِهذَا الْقُرْآنِ وَ الْغَوْا فِيهِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَغْلِبُونَ‌).

This is an ancient method for struggling against the influence of truth, which continues today in a broader and more dangerous form. To divert people's thoughts and to stifle the voices of the callers to truth and justice, they fill the environment with such clamor that no one can hear them. Considering that "alghaw" (والغوا) from the root "laghw" (لغو) has a broad meaning and includes any kind of vain talk, the extent of this program becomes clear.

Sometimes with shouting, clamor, whistling, and hooting.

Sometimes with superstitious and false stories.

Sometimes with romantic and lust-inciting legends! Sometimes it even goes beyond the stage of speech, creating centers of entertainment and corruption, various types of vulgar films, contentless entertaining publications, false political games, and artificial excitements—in short, anything that diverts people's thoughts from the axis of truth.

Worst of all, they sometimes raise vain debates among the scholars of a nation and keep them so occupied with "he-said-she-said" that any opportunity for reflection on fundamental issues is taken away from them.

But were the polytheists able to prevail over the Quran with these actions? No! They and their mischief vanished, and the Quran became more widespread and fruitful day by day, shining throughout the entire world.

The next verse refers to the severe punishment of such individuals, stating: "Most certainly, We shall make the disbelievers—and at the forefront of them, those who prevented people from hearing the Divine verses—taste a severe punishment" (فَلَنُذِيقَنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا عَذاباً شَدِيداً).

This punishment may be in this world in the form of captivity and being killed at the hands of the victorious armies of Islam, or in the Hereafter, or both.

"And We shall certainly requite them for the worst of the deeds they used to do" (وَ لَنَجْزِيَنَّهُمْ أَسْوَأَ الَّذِي كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ‌).

What deed is worse than disbelief, polytheism, denying the Divine verses, and preventing people from hearing the words of truth?

While they will see the punishment for all their deeds, why is the emphasis placed only on "aswa’" (the worst of them)?

This expression may be intended to clarify the subject of punishment with a more serious emphasis and threat, and also to point toward the act of preventing people from hearing the voice of the great Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) of God.

The expression "kanu ya’malun" (كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ‌) is evidence that the emphasis is mostly on deeds they performed continuously; in other words, it was not a momentary slip but a constant program for them.

Then, for further emphasis, He adds: "That is the reward of the enemies of Allah: the Fire of Hell!" (ذلِكَ جَزاءُ أَعْداءِ اللَّهِ النَّارُ) [^1] But not a temporary and fleeting fire; rather, "for them therein is the eternal home" (لَهُمْ فِيها دارُ الْخُلْدِ). Yes, "they are punished with this severe and painful torment because they used to reject Our verses" (جَزاءً بِما كانُوا بِآياتِنا يَجْحَدُونَ‌) [^2]

[^1]: "Al-Nar" (the Fire) may be an "atf al-bayan" (explanatory apposition) or "badal" (substitute) for "jaza’" (reward), or the "khabar" (predicate) for a "mubtada’ mahdhuf" (omitted subject), estimated as "huwa al-nar" (it is the Fire).

[^2]: "Jaza’an" may be the object of an omitted verb "yujzawna jaza’an" (they are rewarded a reward) or a "maf’ul li-ajlih" (object of purpose).

Not only did they reject the Divine verses, but they also prevented others from hearing them.

"Yajhadun" (يجحدون) is from the root "jahd" (جحد). As Raghib says in Al-Mufradat, it originally means denying something that is affirmed in the heart, or affirming something that is denied in the heart; in other words, denying realities while having knowledge of them. This is the worst type of disbelief (for more explanation on this subject, see Volume 15, page 412, under verse 14 of Surah An-Naml).

Since a person, when afflicted by a calamity—especially if it is a hard and heavy one—thinks of the primary cause to find him and take revenge, sometimes wanting to tear the main agent to pieces if he can reach him, the last verse under discussion refers to such a state that arises for the disbelievers in Hell, stating: "The disbelievers will say: 'Our Lord! Show us those among the jinn and men who led us astray, so that we may put them under our feet, to trample them! So that they may be among the lowest of people!'" (وَ قالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا رَبَّنا أَرِنَا الَّذَيْنِ أَضَلَّانا مِنَ الْجِنِّ وَ الْإِنْسِ نَجْعَلْهُما تَحْتَ أَقْدامِنا لِيَكُونا مِنَ الْأَسْفَلِينَ‌).

They were above our heads for a lifetime and dragged us into paths of misery; now our wish is to place them under our feet so that the burning of our hearts may subside! The very ones who told us: "Do not listen to the words of Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله), he is a sorcerer, he is insane, and he speaks deliriously." They created tumult so that we would not hear his voice and his enchanting melody would not affect our hearts. They wove stories of Rostam and Esfandiyar and other legends to keep us entertained. Now we realize that the water of eternal life was flowing in his words, and his melodious tunes, like the breath of the Messiah, brought the dead back to life—but alas, it is now too late.

Undoubtedly, the meaning of "jinn and men" here refers to the groups of devils and devil-like seductive humans, not two specific individuals. The dual form of the verb when the subject refers to two groups is not problematic, just as it appears in the verse: "So which of the favors of your Lord will you [two groups] deny?" (فَبِأَيِّ آلاءِ رَبِّكُما تُكَذِّبانِ‌).

Some commentators have said regarding the phrase "so that they may be among the lowest" (لِيَكُونا مِنَ الْأَسْفَلِينَ‌):

The meaning is that the seducers among jinn and men should be placed in the lowest depths of Hell. However, the apparent meaning is what was mentioned previously: that out of the intensity of their anger, they want these seducers—who held the highest positions in the world—to be placed under the feet of their followers there and hold the lowest rank.

Ayahs

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ قَالُوا۟ رَبُّنَا ٱللَّهُ ثُمَّ ٱسْتَقَـٰمُوا۟ تَتَنَزَّلُ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ أَلَّا تَخَافُوا۟ وَلَا تَحْزَنُوا۟ وَأَبْشِرُوا۟ بِٱلْجَنَّةِ ٱلَّتِى كُنتُمْ تُوعَدُونَ (٣٠)
Indeed those who say, ‘Our Lord is Allah!’ and then remain steadfast, the angels descend upon them, [saying,] ‘Do not fear, nor be grieved! Receive the good news of the paradise, which you have been promised. (30)
نَحْنُ أَوْلِيَآؤُكُمْ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَفِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ۖ وَلَكُمْ فِيهَا مَا تَشْتَهِىٓ أَنفُسُكُمْ وَلَكُمْ فِيهَا مَا تَدَّعُونَ (٣١)
We are your friends in the life of this world and in the Hereafter, and you will have in it whatever your souls desire, and you will have in it whatever you ask for, (31)
نُزُلًا مِّنْ غَفُورٍ رَّحِيمٍ (٣٢)
as a hospitality from One all-forgiving, all-merciful.’ (32)

Commentary

The Descent of Angels upon Steadfast Believers

We know that the Quranic method for explaining concepts is to place opposing matters side by side so that their status becomes clearly illuminated through comparison. Since the preceding verses spoke of stubborn deniers who persisted in their disbelief—and whom Allah threatens with various punishments and retributions—the verses under discussion speak of believers who are firm and unshakable in their faith. Allah mentions seven rewards and blessings He has ordained for them, which are generally the exact opposite of the previous punishments.

First, He says: "Indeed, those who say, 'Our Lord is Allah,' and then remain steadfast, finding not the slightest deviation and demonstrating in word and deed all that this entails, the angels descend upon them, saying: 'Do not fear and do not grieve'" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ قالُوا رَبُّنَا اللَّهُ ثُمَّ اسْتَقامُوا تَتَنَزَّلُ عَلَيْهِمُ الْمَلائِكَةُ أَلَّا تَخافُوا وَ لا تَحْزَنُوا).

What a comprehensive and beautiful expression, which in reality encompasses all virtues and prominent qualities: first, binding the heart to God and attaining firm faith in Him, and then coloring the entirety of one's life with the hue of faith and centering it around that axis. [^1] There are many who claim to love Allah but lack steadfastness in action; they are weak and frail individuals who, when faced with the storm of desires, bid farewell to faith and become polytheists in practice, and when their interests are endangered, they lose even that meager and slight faith.

[^1]: "Istaqamu" is derived from the root "istiqamah," meaning to adhere to the straight path and remain firm on the correct line. Some lexicographers have also interpreted it as "moderation," and combining these two meanings is not far-fetched.

In one of the sermons of Nahj al-Balagha, Imam Ali عليه السلام interprets this verse with an eloquent and meaningful statement. After reciting it, he says:

"You have said, 'Our Lord is Allah,' so remain steadfast upon His Book, upon the path of His command, and upon the righteous way of His worship. Then do not stray from it, do not introduce innovations into it, and never oppose it." [^1] In another tradition from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, we read that he recited this verse and then added:

"People have said these words, then most of them disbelieved. But whoever says them and continues in them until death overtakes him, he is among those who remained steadfast upon them." [^2] And if we see in a tradition narrated from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام that, in response to a question regarding the interpretation of "steadfastness," he said:

"By Allah, it is that which you are upon (the path of Wilayah)," ^3 it does not mean that the concept of the verse is limited to the issue of Wilayah. Rather, because accepting the leadership of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام is the guarantee for the preservation of the line of Monotheism (Tawhid), the authentic method of Islam, and the continuation of righteous deeds, he interpreted steadfastness in this sense.

[^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 176. [^2]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verses discussed.

In short, the value of a human being, which is summarized in faith and righteous deeds, is reflected in this verse in the phrase "they said, 'Our Lord is Allah,' then remained steadfast." Therefore, it is mentioned in a tradition from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله that a person said to him: "Inform me of a matter I may hold fast to (so that I may be saved in this world and the Hereafter)."

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "Say, 'My Lord is Allah,' and then stand firm upon this statement!" Then the man asked: "What is the most dangerous thing I should fear?" The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله held his tongue and said: "This!" [^1] Now let us see what blessings from Allah are bestowed upon those who bring these two great principles to life within themselves.

In these verses, the Quran points to seven great blessings—blessings that are announced to them as glad tidings by the angels of Allah who descend upon them.

Yes, a human being reaches a point where, in the light of faith and steadfastness, angels descend upon him and announce to him the Divine message, which is entirely grace and mercy.

After the first and second glad tidings regarding the absence of "fear" and "grief" which were mentioned:

In the third stage, they say: "And receive the glad tidings of the Paradise which you were promised" (وَ أَبْشِرُوا بِالْجَنَّةِ الَّتِي كُنْتُمْ تُوعَدُونَ).

In the fourth glad tiding, they add: "We are your allies and helpers in the life of this world and in the Hereafter." We will never leave you alone; we help you in good deeds and protect you from slips until you enter Paradise (نَحْنُ أَوْلِياؤُكُمْ فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَ فِي الْآخِرَةِ).

In the fifth glad tiding, they say: In Paradise, whatever you desire of blessings and bounties is provided for you, and there are no conditions or restrictions (وَ لَكُمْ فِيها ما تَشْتَهِي أَنْفُسُكُمْ).

The sixth glad tiding is that not only will material blessings and whatever you desire reach you, but "whatever spiritual blessings you request shall be at your disposal" (وَ لَكُمْ فِيها ما تَدَّعُونَ).

And finally, the seventh and last glad tiding they give them is that you are the guests of Allah in His eternal Paradise, and "all these blessings are bestowed upon you as a hospitality from a Host to a noble guest, from the Forgiving, Merciful Lord" (نُزُلًا مِنْ غَفُورٍ رَحِيمٍ).

Key Points

Within these verses and their short yet meaningful expressions, many subtle points are hidden:

1- Does the descent of angels upon the steadfast believers occur at the time of death and the transition from this world to the next? As a group of commentators have suggested, or do they come to them at three stations: "at the time of death," "at the time of entering the grave," and "at the time of being resurrected on the Day of Judgment"?

Or is it that these glad tidings are permanent and continuous, such that they constantly whisper these truths into the ears of the believers' souls through spiritual inspirations, even though the voice of the angels becomes louder and clearer at the time of death or entry into the arena of Mahshar?

Since the verse has no restrictive conditions, it is more consistent with the latter meaning, especially since the angels say in the fourth glad tiding: "We are your friends in this world and the Hereafter." This is evidence that they hear these tidings from the angels while they are alive in this world—not tidings with tongue and words, but tidings that believers hear with the ears of their souls, feeling them in the depths of their hearts during difficulties and hardships, thereby finding peace.

It is true that in several narrations, this verse is interpreted as the time of death, but in other narrations, it is interpreted in a broader sense that includes the state of life as well. [^1] From the sum of these narrations, it can be concluded that the mention of the specific state of death is a clear instance of this broad and expansive concept, and we know that interpretations found in narrations are often in the form of stating clear examples.

In any case, these are the glad tidings of the Divine angels that radiate within the spirit and soul of the faithful and steadfast person, granting them strength and power during the harsh storms of life, and giving them firmness of step at precipices and places of slipping.

2- What is the difference between "fear" (khawf) and "grief" (huzn)? A group of commentators have said: "Fear" relates to frightening future events, and "grief" relates to unpleasant past events. In this way, the angels say to them: neither be worried about the difficult events you have ahead of you—whether in this world, at the time of death, or in the stages of resurrection—nor let grief enter your heart for your past sins or the children you leave behind in the world.

The precedence of "fear" over "grief" may also be due to the fact that the believer's concern is greater regarding future events, especially the court of the Resurrection.

Some have also said that fear is in the face of punishment, and grief is in the face of the loss of reward, and the angels of Allah make them hopeful of the Lord's grace in both parts.

3- The expression "you were promised" (كُنْتُمْ تُوعَدُونَ) is a comprehensive expression that evokes all the descriptions of Paradise in the minds of the steadfast believers. That is, Paradise with all the qualities you have heard of—with its maidens and palaces, with its spiritual and psychological blessings, and with the highly precious bounties that, according to the Quran, no one knows of and has not crossed anyone's mind: "No soul knows what has been hidden for them of comfort for the eyes" (Surah As-Sajdah, 32:17)—all of it is at your disposal.

4- In the fourth glad tiding, the angels introduce themselves as the allies and helpers of the believers in this world and the Hereafter, which is in fact the opposite of the previous verses where the faithless disbelievers cry out against their misguided and seductive leaders and want to take revenge on these impure ones in Hell.

5- The difference between the fifth and sixth glad tidings is that in the fifth, they are told that whatever their hearts desire is there, and their desiring it and its being provided are one and the same. However, we know that the expression "what your souls desire" (tash-tahi anfusukum) is usually used for material pleasures, while "what you call for" (ma tadda'un) means spiritual requests and psychological blessings and pleasures. In short, everything is gathered there, and every spiritual and material blessing they want is provided.

6- "Nuzul," as we have said before, means the provisions with which a guest is entertained, and some have interpreted it as the very first means of hospitality for a guest. In any case, this subtle and beautiful expression shows that the steadfast believers are all guests of Allah, Paradise is the guest-house of Allah, and its blessings are the means of entertaining the friends of Allah.

7- By reflecting on the depth of these concepts and the greatness of these Divine promises given to the believers through the angels, the human spirit takes flight and his entire being is drawn toward faith and steadfastness. It was in the light of this culture and these teachings that Islam transformed a handful of ignorant Arabs into exemplary human beings who did not hesitate to make any kind of sacrifice or selflessness. These same teachings are what can inspire Muslims today on the path to victory over all problems.

Of course, it should not be forgotten that "steadfastness," like "righteous deeds," is the fruit of the tree of "faith." For when faith attains sufficient depth and influence, it will invite the person to steadfastness, just as steadfastness in the path of truth increases the depth of faith; these two have a reciprocal effect.

It is also understood from other verses of the Quran that faith and steadfastness not only cause spiritual blessings to flow toward man, but he will also benefit from the material blessings of this world under the shadow of these two. In Surah Al-Jinn, verse 16, we read:

"And [Allah revealed] that if they had remained steadfast on the way, We would have given them abundant water to drink" (and We would have granted them years of abundant rain and blessing).

[^1]: Refer to Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, pp. 546-547, narrations 38, 40, 45, and 46. [^1]: Ruh al-Bayan, Vol. 8, p. 254.

Ayahs

وَمَنْ أَحْسَنُ قَوْلًا مِّمَّن دَعَآ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ وَعَمِلَ صَـٰلِحًا وَقَالَ إِنَّنِى مِنَ ٱلْمُسْلِمِينَ (٣٣)
Who has a better call than him who summons to Allah and acts righteously and says, ‘Indeed I am one of the Muslims’? (33)
وَلَا تَسْتَوِى ٱلْحَسَنَةُ وَلَا ٱلسَّيِّئَةُ ۚ ٱدْفَعْ بِٱلَّتِى هِىَ أَحْسَنُ فَإِذَا ٱلَّذِى بَيْنَكَ وَبَيْنَهُۥ عَدَٰوَةٌ كَأَنَّهُۥ وَلِىٌّ حَمِيمٌ (٣٤)
Good and evil [conduct] are not equal. Repel [evil] with what is best. [If you do so,] he between whom and you was enmity, will then be as though he were a sympathetic friend. (34)
وَمَا يُلَقَّىٰهَآ إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ صَبَرُوا۟ وَمَا يُلَقَّىٰهَآ إِلَّا ذُو حَظٍّ عَظِيمٍ (٣٥)
But none is granted it except those who are patient, and none is granted it except the greatly endowed. (35)
وَإِمَّا يَنزَغَنَّكَ مِنَ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ نَزْغٌ فَٱسْتَعِذْ بِٱللَّهِ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ (٣٦)
Should an incitement from Satan prompt you [to ill feeling], seek the protection of Allah. Indeed, He is the All-hearing, the All-knowing. (36)

Commentary

Repel Evil with Good!

In the preceding verses, the discussion concerned those who forbade people from listening to the verses of the Quran—namely, the callers toward deviation and misguidance.

However, the verses under discussion speak of their polar opposite, whose speech is the best of speech. It says: "And who is better in speech than one who invites to Allah and does righteous deeds and says, 'Indeed, I am of the Muslims' and has accepted Islam with his entire being?" (وَ مَنْ أَحْسَنُ قَوْلًا مِمَّنْ دَعا إِلَى اللَّهِ وَ عَمِلَ صالِحاً وَ قالَ إِنَّنِي مِنَ الْمُسْلِمِينَ‌).

Although the verse is in the form of a question, it is clearly a rhetorical question of negation; meaning, no one’s speech is better than that of the callers toward Allah and the heralds of Monotheism—those heralds who emphasize and solidify their verbal invitation with their righteous deeds, and validate their righteous actions through their belief in Islam and submission to the Truth.

This verse explicitly introduces the best speakers as those possessing these three attributes: inviting to Allah, righteous action, and submission to the Truth.

In reality, such individuals, in addition to the three well-known pillars of faith—verbal profession, acting upon the pillars (of faith), and belief in the heart—have also grasped a fourth pillar: the propagation and dissemination of the true religion, establishing proof for the foundations of faith, and removing the traces of doubt and hesitation from the hearts of Allah's servants. With these four attributes, these heralds are the best heralds in the world.

Although a group of commentators has applied these attributes to the person of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, or the Prophet and the Imams who invited toward the Truth, or specifically to the Muadhins (callers to prayer), it is evident that the verse has a broad and expansive meaning that encompasses all heralds of Monotheism who possess these qualities. Certainly, its supreme manifestation is the person of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله (especially considering the time of the verse's revelation), and in the next degree, the Infallible Imams علیهم السلام, and after them, all scholars, thinkers, mujahideen in the path of Truth, those who enjoin good and forbid evil, and the propagators of Islam from every stratum and group. This verse is a great glad tiding and a unique honor for all those who can find encouragement in it.

And if it has been said that this verse praises "Bilal al-Habashi," the special Muadhin of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, it is because he raised the melody of Monotheism during a dark and terrifying era, made his life a shield for it, and completed these attributes with firm faith, rare steadfastness, righteous deeds, and continuity on the correct path of Islam! The phrase "and says, 'Indeed, I am of the Muslims'" (وقالَ إِنَّنِي مِنَ الْمُسْلِمِينَ‌) has been interpreted in two ways: First, that "said" (qala) here is from the root of "speech" (qawl) meaning belief; i.e., he has a firm belief in Islam.

Second, that "speech" here carries its literal meaning of "speaking"; i.e., he cries out with pride and honor in the pure religion of God: "I am of the Muslims!" The first meaning is more appropriate, though combining both within the concept of the verse is possible.

After stating the invitation toward God and the attributes of the callers to Allah, it explains the method of invitation, saying: "The good deed and the evil deed are not equal" (لا تَسْتَوِي الْحَسَنَةُ وَ لَا السَّيِّئَةُ) [1]. While the opponents of Truth have no weapon other than slander, defamation, mockery, ridicule,

[1]: The repetition of "no" (la) in the phrase "nor the evil" (wa la al-sayyi'ah) is for the emphasis of negation.

and various forms of pressure and oppression, your weapon must be purity, piety, truthful speech, gentleness, and love.

Indeed, the school of misguidance approves of nothing but such tools, while the school of Truth utilizes only such means.

Although "good" (hasanah) and "evil" (sayyi'ah) have broad meanings—all goodness, virtues, blessings, and graces are gathered in the concept of hasanah, just as every form of deviation, ugliness, and punishment is summarized in the concept of sayyi'ah—in the verse under discussion, that branch of hasanah and sayyi'ah related to methods of propagation is intended.

However, a group of commentators has interpreted hasanah as Islam and Monotheism, and sayyi'ah as polytheism and disbelief.

Some have interpreted hasanah as righteous deeds and sayyi'ah as foul deeds; others have interpreted hasanah as high human qualities such as patience, forbearance, tolerance, and forgiveness, and sayyi'ah as anger, ignorance, violence, and vengefulness.

Yet, the first interpretation seems most appropriate of all.

In a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read regarding the interpretation of the above verse:

"The good deed (hasanah) is Taqiyyah (precautionary dissimulation), and the evil deed (sayyi'ah) is disclosure." Of course, this hadith refers to a situation where disclosing one's belief would lead to the waste of resources and the destruction of plans and goals [1]. Then, to complete this statement, He adds: "Repel [evil] by that [deed] which is better" (ادْفَعْ بِالَّتِي هِيَ أَحْسَنُ‌).

Repel falsehood by means of the Truth, and ignorance and violence with forbearance and tolerance, and confront aggression with pardon and forgiveness. Never respond to evil with evil, or to ugliness with

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verses discussed.

ugliness, for this is the method of the vengeful and causes the obstinacy and stubbornness of the misguided.

At the end of the verse, He points to the deep philosophy of this program in a short sentence, saying: The result of this action will be that staunch enemies become like warm and intimate friends (فَإِذَا الَّذِي بَيْنَكَ وَ بَيْنَهُ عَداوَةٌ كَأَنَّهُ وَلِيٌّ حَمِيمٌ‌).

What the Quran has stated in this verse—and which appears in another form in Surah Al-Mu'minun, verse 96 (ادْفَعْ بِالَّتِي هِيَ أَحْسَنُ السَّيِّئَةَ)—is among the most important, delicate, and fruitful methods of propagation, especially against ignorant and stubborn enemies, and the latest psychological research has also arrived at this conclusion.

For anyone who does evil expects a reciprocal response, especially bad people because they themselves are of this sort, and sometimes they respond to one evil several times over. When they see that the opposing side not only does not respond to evil with evil but confronts it with goodness and kindness, a storm arises within them; their conscience is placed under severe pressure and awakens; a revolution takes place within their soul; they become ashamed, feel small, and attribute greatness to the other party.

It is here that grudges and enmities are uprooted by a storm from within the soul, and love and sincerity take their place.

Naturally, this is a general rule, not a permanent one, for there is always a minority who abuse this method and do not become human or cease their foul deeds until they are placed under the crushing blows of the whip of punishment.

Of course, the account of this group is separate, and severity must be used against them, but it must not be forgotten that this group is always in the minority; the law that governs the majority is the law of "repelling evil with good."

Therefore, we observe that the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله and the Infallible Leaders علیهم السلام always utilized this sublime Quranic method. For example, during the Conquest of Mecca, when not only enemies but even friends expected severe vengeance from the Muslims and a bloodbath in that land of disbelief, polytheism, and hypocrisy—the center of cold-hearted and ruthless enemies—and even some of the standard-bearers of the Islamic army that day turned toward Abu Sufyan and chanted: "Today is the day of slaughter! Today sanctity is violated! Today Allah has humiliated the Quraysh!" The Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, with the phrase "Go, for you are the freed ones" (Idhhabu fa antum al-tulaqa), included everyone in his pardon. He turned toward Abu Sufyan and changed that vengeful slogan into this affectionate one: "Today is the day of mercy! Today Allah has honored the Quraysh!" [1]. This very act created such a storm in the hearts of the polytheistic Meccans that, as the Quran says: "they enter the religion of Allah in multitudes" (يَدْخُلُونَ فِي دِينِ اللَّهِ أَفْواجاً)—they became Muslims in waves and accepted the faith of Islam with heart and soul (Surah An-Nasr, verse 2).

Yet, despite all this, as recorded in Islamic histories, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله named a few individuals and excluded them from the general amnesty because they were dangerous and unforgivable individuals; but he included everyone else except those few in the general pardon, and meanwhile expressed this meaningful sentence: "I say regarding you the same as Yusuf said regarding his brothers who had oppressed him: 'No blame will there be upon you today. Allah will forgive you; and He is the most merciful of the merciful'" (لا تَثْرِيبَ عَلَيْكُمُ الْيَوْمَ يَغْفِرُ اللَّهُ لَكُمْ وَ هُوَ أَرْحَمُ الرَّاحِمِينَ‌) [1]. "Wali" here means friend, and "Hamim" originally means hot and scalding water; if sweat is called hamim, it is because of its warmth, and the bathhouse (hammam) is called so for the same reason. Highly affectionate, warm, and "hot" friends are also called "Hamim," and this is what is intended in the verse.

It is noteworthy that He says: "as if he were a devoted friend" (كَأَنَّهُ وَلِيٌّ حَمِيمٌ‌), indicating that even if he does not truly enter the ranks of intimate friends, he will at least appear so outwardly.

Since such an encounter with opponents is not a simple or easy task, and reaching such a station requires deep moral self-building, in the next verse, He explains the moral foundations of this type of encounter with enemies in a short and meaningful phrase, saying: "And none is granted it except those who are patient and steadfast" (وَ ما يُلَقَّاها إِلَّا الَّذِينَ صَبَرُوا) 2 "and none is granted it except one possessing a great portion [of faith, piety, and character]" (وَ ما يُلَقَّاها إِلَّا ذُو حظٍّ عَظِيمٍ‌).

Yes, a person must engage in self-building for a long time to be able to overcome his anger and rage. In the light of faith and piety, his soul must become so expansive and strong that it is not easily affected by the harassment of enemies, and the sense of vengeance does not ignite within him. A great soul and sufficient "expansion of the chest" (sharh al-sadr) are necessary for a person to reach such a stage of human perfection where he responds to evil with good, and for the sake of Allah and to reach

[1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 21, p. 132. 2: The pronoun in yulaqqaha refers to the "trait" or "exhortation" understood from the previous sentence.

his sacred goals, he even goes beyond the stage of pardon and forgiveness and reaches the station of "repelling evil with good"!

Here again, we encounter the issue of "patience" (sabr), which is the root of all virtuous moral traits and material and spiritual progress and success [1].

And since there are obstacles on the path to reaching this great goal, and satanic whisperings in various forms hinder humans, in the final verse under discussion, He addresses the person of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله as a role model, saying: "And if there comes to you from Satan an evil suggestion, then seek refuge in Allah. Indeed, He is the Hearing, the Knowing" (وَ إِمَّا يَنْزَغَنَّكَ مِنَ الشَّيْطانِ نَزْغٌ فَاسْتَعِذْ بِاللَّهِ إِنَّهُ هُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْعَلِيمُ‌) 2. "Nazgh" means "entering into a matter with the intent of corruption," and for this reason, satanic whisperings are called "Nazgh."

This warning is actually because in such moments, certain thoughts usually cross the mind, or so-called "expedient-thinking" individuals offer recommendations such as: "People cannot be reformed except with force," "Blood must be washed with blood,"

[1]: A group of commentators has considered the phrase "none is granted it except one possessing a great portion" as a reference to the great rewards of such noble and forgiving humans in the Hereafter, but considering that the verse is in the process of explaining the moral foundations of this great act, the aforementioned interpretation seems unlikely.

"Pity for the sharp-toothed leopard is oppression to the sheep," and the like. With these whisperings, they want to recommend retaliation everywhere and respond to evil with evil.

The Quran says: Beware of getting caught in these whisperings, and do not rely on violence except in specific and exceptional cases; and whenever you are faced with such talk, seek refuge in God and trust in Him, for He hears all words and is aware of everyone's intentions.

Of course, the above verse has a broad meaning and says: one must seek refuge in God against all satanic whisperings, but what was mentioned is one of its clear manifestations.

Key Points

1- The Four-Stage Program for Callers to Allah

In the four verses above, four discussions regarding the invitation toward God are presented: First: Self-building of the callers in terms of faith and righteous action. Second: Utilizing the method of "repelling evil with good." Third: Providing the moral foundations for performing this method. Fourth: Removing obstacles from the path and fighting against satanic whisperings.

The Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله and the Infallible Imams were themselves the best role models for this program, and one of the reasons for the rapid progress of Islam in that dark environment full of ignorance was the use of this very program.

Today, psychologists have written books and treatises on the way to influence others, which seem insignificant compared to the greatness of the above verses, especially since the methods they recommend often have an aspect of superficiality, making others look foolish, and sometimes trickery and deception, whereas the above Quranic method, which is based on faith, piety, and authenticity, is free from all these things.

How good it would be if Muslims today revived this method and expanded the reach of Islam in a world that thirsts for it through this path.

Interestingly, in a hadith found in the Tafsir of "Ali ibn Ibrahim," we read: "Allah disciplined His Prophet and said: 'The good deed and the evil deed are not equal; repel [evil] by that [deed] which is better.' He said: Repel the evil of one who has done evil to you with your good deed, until the one between whom and you there is enmity becomes as if he were a devoted friend" [1].

2- Man in the Face of the Storm of Whisperings

On the long path that man takes toward happiness and gaining the pleasure of God, there are difficult passes where devils lie in ambush, and if man remains alone, he will never have the ability to traverse this path. He must grasp the mantle of Divine grace and, by relying and trusting in Him, traverse this perilous road. Whenever the storms become stronger and stronger, he should seek refuge more in the shadow of God's grace.

In a hadith, we read that in the presence of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, one person spoke ill of another, and the fire of anger was ignited in his heart. The Prophet said: "I know a word which, if he said it,

[1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 549.

his anger would depart: 'I seek refuge in Allah from the accursed Satan' (A'udhu billahi min al-shaytan al-rajim)." The angry man said: "Do you see me as a madman?" (Meaning: Do you think I am crazy and Satan has entered my skin?). The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله cited the Quran and recited this verse: "And if there comes to you from Satan an evil suggestion, then seek refuge in Allah" (وَ إِمَّا يَنْزَغَنَّكَ مِنَ الشَّيْطانِ نَزْغٌ فَاسْتَعِذْ بِاللَّهِ‌) [1], indicating that the storm of anger is from the whisperings of Satan, just as the storm of lust and passion are each among those whisperings.

In the book Al-Khisal, we read: The Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, taught his companions four hundred chapters regarding matters that benefit the religion and worldly life of Muslims, and among them was this: "When Satan whispers to one of you, let him seek refuge in Allah and say: 'I believe in Allah, making the religion sincere for Him'" 2.

[1]: Ruh al-Ma'ani, Vol. 24, p. 111. 2: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 551.

Ayahs

وَمِنْ ءَايَـٰتِهِ ٱلَّيْلُ وَٱلنَّهَارُ وَٱلشَّمْسُ وَٱلْقَمَرُ ۚ لَا تَسْجُدُوا۟ لِلشَّمْسِ وَلَا لِلْقَمَرِ وَٱسْجُدُوا۟ لِلَّهِ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَهُنَّ إِن كُنتُمْ إِيَّاهُ تَعْبُدُونَ (٣٧)
Among His signs are night and day, and the sun and the moon. Do not prostrate to the sun, nor to the moon, but prostrate to Allah who created them, if it is Him that you worship. (37)
فَإِنِ ٱسْتَكْبَرُوا۟ فَٱلَّذِينَ عِندَ رَبِّكَ يُسَبِّحُونَ لَهُۥ بِٱلَّيْلِ وَٱلنَّهَارِ وَهُمْ لَا يَسْـَٔمُونَ ۩ (٣٨)
But if they disdain [the worship of Allah], those who are near your Lord glorify Him night and day, and they are not wearied. (38)
وَمِنْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦٓ أَنَّكَ تَرَى ٱلْأَرْضَ خَـٰشِعَةً فَإِذَآ أَنزَلْنَا عَلَيْهَا ٱلْمَآءَ ٱهْتَزَّتْ وَرَبَتْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَحْيَاهَا لَمُحْىِ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰٓ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (٣٩)
Among His signs, is that you see the earth desolate; but when We send down water upon it, it stirs and swells. Indeed, He who revives it will also revive the dead. Indeed, He has power over all things. (39)

Commentary

Prostrate Only Before Allah

These verses, in fact, initiate a new chapter in this Surah regarding Monotheism (Tawhid) and the Resurrection (Ma'ad), as well as explaining the Prophethood and the greatness of the Quran. They represent a clear manifestation of the "Call to Allah" in contrast to the polytheists who called toward idols.

It begins first with the issue of Monotheism, inviting people toward Allah through the "Signs of the Horizons" (Ayat al-Afaq), stating: "And of His signs are the night and the day and the sun and the moon" [^1] (وَ مِنْ آياتِهِ اللَّيْلُ وَ النَّهارُ وَ الشَّمْسُ وَ الْقَمَرُ).

The night is a source of tranquility, and the light of day is a means of activity and movement. Together, these two turn the wheels of human life in a regular and alternating cycle. If either were eternal or even prolonged, the life of all living beings would be subject to annihilation. For this reason, the moon—whose nights are equivalent to 15 Earth days and its days are of the same length—is by no means habitable; for in its cold and dark nights, everything freezes, and in its scorching days, everything catches fire. Consequently, the survival of living beings like humans there is impossible.

However, the sun is the source of all material blessings in our system. Light, heat, movement, activity, the falling of rain, the growth of plants, and the ripening of fruits—even the beautiful colors of flowers—all stem from the radiance of its existence.

The moon, too, is the illuminator of dark nights, a bright and beautiful lamp for travelers of the deserts and those lost in the wilderness, and through its tides, it creates numerous blessings.

Yet, because of these blessings, a group used to prostrate before these two bright celestial bodies and worship them. They remained halted at the level of "causes" (asbab) without seeing the "Causer of Causes" (Musabbib al-Asbab).

Therefore, immediately after this explanation, the Quran says: "Do not prostrate to the sun or to the moon, but prostrate to Allah, who created them, if it should be Him that you worship" (لا تَسْجُدُوا لِلشَّمْسِ وَ لا لِلْقَمَرِ وَ اسْجُدُوا لِلَّهِ الَّذِي خَلَقَهُنَّ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ إِيَّاهُ تَعْبُدُونَ). [^1] Why do you not go to the source of these blessings? Why do you not rub your forehead at His threshold? Why do you worship beings that are themselves captives to the laws of creation, subject to rising and setting, and prone to various changes?

One must seek Him who is the Ruler and Creator of these laws, who never sets or fades, and whose threshold of Majesty is never reached by the hand of change or alteration.

In this way, it negates one of the widespread branches of polytheism and idolatry—the worship of various natural entities that possess benefits—and sends a message to all: go toward the Creator of these beings and do not stop at the effect; seek the Cause of Causes.

In reality, in this verse, the unified system governing the sun, moon, night, and day is used as an argument for the existence of the One God, and His status as Creator and Ruler is used as evidence for the necessity of His worship.

The phrase "if it should be Him that you worship" (إِنْ كُنْتُمْ إِيَّاهُ تَعْبُدُونَ) actually points to the fact that if you intend to worship Allah, you must exclude all others and not associate anything with Him in worship, for His worship can never be combined with the worship of another.

Then it adds: If this logical reasoning does not affect their minds and they still pursue idols and metaphorical deities, forgetting the True Deity, "if they are arrogant" regarding the worship of Allah, do not be concerned, for the near-stationed angels who are in His presence "exalt Him by night and by day, and they do not tire" (فَإِنِ اسْتَكْبَرُوا فَالَّذِينَ عِنْدَ رَبِّكَ يُسَبِّحُونَ لَهُ بِاللَّيْلِ وَ النَّهارِ وَ هُمْ لا يَسْأَمُونَ). [^1] If a group of ignorant and unaware people do not prostrate before His Pure Essence, it is of no consequence; this vast universe is full of near-stationed angels who are constantly in bowing, prostration, praise, and glorification. Furthermore, He has no need for their worship; rather, they are in need of worshipping Him, for every honor and perfection for contingent beings exists under the shadow of servitude to Him.

As we mentioned, the above verses are among the "Verses of Prostration" (Ayat al-Sajdah). However, there is a discussion among Sunni jurists as to whether the prostration is obligatory after the beginning of the first verse (the phrase ta'budun) or after the completion of both verses (the phrase wa hum la yas'amun). A group has accepted the former, such as Shafi'i and Malik, while some have preferred the latter, such as Abu Hanifa and Ahmad ibn Hanbal.

However, according to the Imami (Shi'a) scholars, based on narrations received from the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام, the place of prostration is the phrase ta'budun, and this is one of the obligatory prostrations of the Quran.

It is also necessary to note that what is obligatory is the act of prostration itself, while the recitation (dhikr) during it is recommended (mustahabb). In a narration, we read that one should say this dhikr in the prostration:

"لا اله الا اللَّه حقا حقا، لا اله الا اللَّه ايمانا و تصديقا، لا اله الا اللَّه عبودية و رقا سجدت لك يا رب تعبدا و رقا، لا مستنكفا و لا مستكبرا بل انا عبد ذليل خائف مستجير" [^1] (There is no god but Allah, truly, truly; there is no god but Allah, in faith and sincerity; there is no god but Allah, in servitude and bondage. I have prostrated to You, O Lord, in worship and slavery, neither disdainful nor arrogant, but rather I am a lowly, fearful, and seeking-refuge servant.)

Once again, it returns to the verses of Monotheism which pave the way for the issue of the Resurrection. If previously the talk was of the sun, moon, and celestial signs, here the talk is of terrestrial and earthly signs.

It says: "And of His signs is that you see the earth hushed, but when We send down upon it water, it quivers and grows" (وَ مِنْ آياتِهِ أَنَّكَ تَرَى الْأَرْضَ خاشِعَةً فَإِذا أَنْزَلْنا عَلَيْهَا الْماءَ اهْتَزَّتْ وَ رَبَتْ).

Where is the dry, dead, and motionless earth, and where are all these signs of life and its various manifestations? What power is it that, with the descent of a few drops of rain, creates all this movement, activity, and life from dead soil? This is one of the signs of the infinite knowledge and power of the Lord and the indicators of His Generous Existence.

Then, from this clear monotheistic issue—namely the issue of "life," whose secrets are still not fully discovered by the greatest scientists—it moves with a swift and beautiful transition to the issue of the Resurrection, saying: "Indeed, He who has given it life is the Giver of Life to the dead" on the Day of Judgment! (إِنَّ الَّذِي أَحْياها لَمُحْيِ الْمَوْتى‌).

Yes, "Indeed, He is over all things competent" (إِنَّهُ عَلى‌ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ قَدِيرٌ).

The proofs of His power are visible everywhere, and every year you see its signs with your own eyes. In spite of this, how can you doubt the issue of the Resurrection and consider it among the impossibilities? What ignorance, negligence, and unawareness!

"Khashi'ah" (خاشعة) is from the root khushu', which originally means supplication and humility combined with etiquette. Using this expression for parched earth is, in fact, a type of metaphor. Indeed, when the earth becomes parched and lacks water, it becomes void of any flowers or plants, appearing like a fallen person or a lifeless corpse. But the descent of rain grants it a new life, causing it to move, increase, and grow.

The word "rabat" (ربت) is from the root rabw, meaning increase and growth. Riba (usury) is also from this root, because the usurer takes his claim with an increase.

"Ihtazzat" (اهتزت) is from the root hazz, meaning intense movement or quivering. Regarding the methods of proving the physical Resurrection and how to argue for this issue from the world of plants, we have had a more detailed discussion at the end of Surah Ya-Sin.

[^1]: Note that this verse is one of the verses where prostration is obligatory upon reciting or hearing it. [^1]: Here, the feminine plural pronoun in "khalaqahunna" refers to the night, day, sun, and moon. According to linguists and commentators, the feminine plural pronoun for rational beings sometimes refers to non-rational plurals as well... Some also believe this pronoun refers to "signs" (ayat), which is also a non-rational feminine plural. Some have also suggested it refers only to the sun and moon in consideration of their genus which includes all stars, as if attributing intellect and consciousness to them. Additionally, the phrase "fa-in istakbaru" is a conditional sentence whose result (jaza) is omitted, implied as: "If they are arrogant toward the worship of Allah and His Oneness, it does not harm Him at all."
