Ayahs

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يُلْحِدُونَ فِىٓ ءَايَـٰتِنَا لَا يَخْفَوْنَ عَلَيْنَآ ۗ أَفَمَن يُلْقَىٰ فِى ٱلنَّارِ خَيْرٌ أَم مَّن يَأْتِىٓ ءَامِنًا يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ۚ ٱعْمَلُوا۟ مَا شِئْتُمْ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ (٤٠)
Those who abuse Our signs are not hidden from Us. Is someone who is cast in the Fire better off, or someone who arrives safely on the Day of Resurrection? Act as you wish; indeed He watches what you do. (40)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بِٱلذِّكْرِ لَمَّا جَآءَهُمْ ۖ وَإِنَّهُۥ لَكِتَـٰبٌ عَزِيزٌ (٤١)
Those who reject the Reminder when it comes to them... Indeed, it is an august Book: (41)
لَّا يَأْتِيهِ ٱلْبَـٰطِلُ مِنۢ بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَلَا مِنْ خَلْفِهِۦ ۖ تَنزِيلٌ مِّنْ حَكِيمٍ حَمِيدٍ (٤٢)
falsehood cannot approach it, at present or in future, a [revelation gradually] sent down from One all-wise, all-laudable. (42)

Commentary

The Distorters of the Signs of Truth!

Following the presentation of Divine verses and the signs of the Lord in the preceding verses, the discourse now turns to a threat against those who distort the signs of Monotheism and engage in deceiving and misleading the people. It says: "Indeed, those who distort Our signs are not hidden from Us" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يُلْحِدُونَ فِي آياتِنا لا يَخْفَوْنَ عَلَيْنا).

They may deceive people through fallacy and sophistry, and they may cast a veil over their ugly and shameful deeds to hide themselves from public view, but they can never hide even their smallest action from Us.

"Yulhidun" (يلحدون) is derived from the root "Ilhad" (الحاد), which originally comes from "Lahd" (لحد). It refers to a hole or niche made in one side [of a trench], which is why the niche placed in one side of a grave is called a "Lahd." Subsequently, any action that deviates from the middle path toward extremes of excess or deficiency is called "Ilhad." The application of this word to "polytheism, idol worship, disbelief, and irreligion" is also due to this connection.

The meaning of "Ilhad in the signs of Allah" is creating doubts regarding the proofs of Monotheism and the Resurrection—which were mentioned in the previous verses under the heading "And among His signs"—or it refers to all Divine signs, whether the cosmic signs mentioned previously or the legislative signs revealed in the Holy Quran and Heavenly Books.

The materialistic and atheistic schools of the modern world, which sometimes introduce religion as a product of ignorance and fear, and sometimes as a result of economic factors or other material matters to divert the people of the world from Monotheism and the Resurrection, are undoubtedly among those included in this verse.

The Quran describes the punishment for all of them in the continuation of this discussion through a clear comparison, saying: "Is he who is cast into the Fire better, or he who comes secure on the Day of Resurrection?" (أَ فَمَنْ يُلْقى‌ فِي النَّارِ خَيْرٌ أَمْ مَنْ يَأْتِي آمِناً يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ).

Those who set fire to the beliefs and faith of the people by creating doubt and corruption must become the prey of the Fire on that Day. And those who, under the shadow of faith, created an environment of peace and security for human society must live in ultimate security on the Day of Resurrection. Does not every one of our deeds manifest itself on that Day?

Although some commentators have interpreted this part of the verse as referring to "Abu Jahl" and his opposite "Hamzah" or "Ammar ibn Yasir," it is evident that this is merely an application (tatbiq). The verse has a broad meaning that includes all of them and others besides them.

It is interesting that regarding the people of Hell, it uses the expression "cast" (yulqa), which indicates they have no choice of their own there. However, regarding the people of Paradise, it uses the expression "comes" (ya'ti), which indicates respect and their freedom of will in choosing security and tranquility.

Furthermore, while Paradise should have been placed in contrast to Hell, here "security" from punishment is placed in contrast to "Hell," pointing out that the most important issue on that Day is this very "security."

And since when one despairs of someone's guidance, they leave him to himself and say: "Do whatever you want," the verse continues by addressing them: "Do what you will!" (اعْمَلُوا ما شِئْتُمْ‌).

"But know that Allah is Seeing of what you do" (إِنَّهُ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ).

It is obvious that this command does not mean they have freedom of action or an obligation to do anything, but rather it is a threat against those into whose ears no word of truth enters. It is a meaningful threat accompanied by a promise of punishment, for seeing the deeds and keeping their account is for this very purpose.

The next verse shifts the discourse from Monotheism and Resurrection to the Quran and Prophethood, and again, as a warning to the stubborn and illogical disbelievers, it says: "Indeed, those who disbelieve in the Reminder (the Holy Quran) when it comes to them [are not hidden from Us]" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بِالذِّكْرِ لَمَّا جاءَهُمْ‌) [1]. The application of "Reminder" (Dhikr) to the "Quran" is because, before anything else, it reminds and awakens man, and it explains with clarity and detail the truths that man has perceived generally through his God-given nature. Similar expressions appear in other verses of the Quran; for instance, in Surah Al-Hijr, verse 9, we read: "Indeed, it is We who sent down the Reminder and indeed, We will be its guardian" (إِنَّا نَحْنُ نَزَّلْنَا الذِّكْرَ وَ إِنَّا لَهُ لَحافِظُونَ‌).

Then, to describe the greatness of the Quran, it adds: "And indeed, it is a mighty Book" (وَ إِنَّهُ لَكِتابٌ عَزِیزٌ).

It is a book that no one can bring the like of or overcome. It is a peerless book; its logic is firm and eloquent, its arguments are strong and powerful, its expressions are cohesive and deep, its teachings are rooted and rich, and its laws and commands are harmonious with the real needs of human beings in all dimensions of life.

[1] Regarding what the predicate (khabar) of "Inna alladhina" is, commentators have offered many possibilities. The most appropriate is that the predicate is the phrase "are not hidden from Us" (la yakhfawna 'alayna), which is omitted due to the context of the previous verse. Some have taken the predicate to be "they will be cast into the Fire," inferred from the previous verse, and some consider it to be the sentence "Those will be called from a far place" which appears in future verses. However, the first meaning seems most appropriate.

Then, it proceeds to a significant and eloquent description of the greatness of this Heavenly Book, saying: "Falsehood cannot come to it from before it or from behind it" (لا يَأْتِيهِ الْباطِلُ مِنْ بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَ لا مِنْ خَلْفِهِ‌).

Because it is "a revelation from a Wise, Praiseworthy [Lord]" (تَنْزِيلٌ مِنْ حَكِيمٍ حَمِيدٍ).

A Lord whose actions are based on wisdom and are in ultimate perfection and correctness, and therefore He is worthy of all praise and glorification.

In the commentary of the phrase "Falsehood cannot come to it...", commentators have said much, the most comprehensive of which is: "No kind of falsehood, from any perspective, and through any path, approaches the Quran." Meaning:

  • There is no contradiction in its concepts.
  • Nothing from previous books and sciences stands against it, nor will future scientific discoveries oppose it.
  • No one can invalidate its truths, nor will it be abrogated in the future.
  • There is no falsehood in its knowledge, laws, exhortations, and reports, nor will any falsehood be discovered later.
  • Not a verse, nor even a word, has been subtracted from it, nor has anything been added to it. In other words, the hands of distorters have been and are kept away from its lofty hem.

In reality, this verse is another expression of verse 9 of Surah Al-Hijr: "Indeed, it is We who sent down the Reminder and indeed, We will be its guardian" (إِنَّا نَحْنُ نَزَّلْنَا الذِّكْرَ وَ إِنَّا لَهُ لَحافِظُونَ‌). [1]

[1] This interpretation was chosen in a brief and general way by Zamakhshari in Al-Kashshaf; the words of Allameh Tabataba'i in Tafsir al-Mizan are similar. Meanwhile, many commentators have either limited the word "falsehood" (batil) or taken it to mean Satan, distorters, lies, or the like. In a Hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام and Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, it is stated: "There is no falsehood in its reports of what has passed, nor is there falsehood in its reports of what will be in the future" (Majma' al-Bayan, under the discussed verses). We know that all of these are expressions of specific instances (masadiq) of the broad and expansive concept of the above verse (reflect on this).

From what we have said, it can be concluded that the phrase "from before it or from behind it" (مِنْ بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَ لا مِنْ خَلْفِهِ‌) is a metaphor for being all-encompassing. That is, from no side, direction, or aspect has falsehood or corruption approached this great Book, nor will it ever. However, some have taken it as a metaphor for "the present time" and "the future time," which is in fact an instance of the first broad concept.

The word "Batil" (falsehood), as Raghib says in Al-Mufradat, is the opposite of "Haqq" (truth). However, it has sometimes been interpreted as one of its instances, such as polytheism, Satan, a perishing being, or a sorcerer. The reason a brave person or hero is called "Batal" is that he nullifies (yubtilu) his opponents and removes them from the field or kills them.

In any case, the appearance of the verse is absolute, and the concept of "falsehood" cannot be limited to a specific instance.

The final phrase of the verse, "a revelation from a Wise, Praiseworthy [Lord]" (تَنْزِيلٌ مِنْ حَكِيمٍ حَمِيدٍ), is in fact a clear and eloquent proof for the impossibility of falsehood finding its way into the Holy Quran in any shape or form. This is because falsehood finds its way into speech that originates from a person with limited knowledge and finite perfections. But for One whose knowledge and wisdom are unlimited and who encompasses all perfections that make Him worthy of praise, no contradiction or discrepancy finds its way into His speech, nor is the line of abrogation or invalidation drawn over it. The hand of distortion is not extended toward it, nor does it have any conflict with the truths of previous books or scientific discoveries in the present or future.

In any case, this verse is among the clear verses that negate any kind of distortion—whether by subtraction or addition—from the Holy Quran. (A further explanation regarding the non-distortion of the Quran was provided in Volume 11, page 18 onwards, under verse 9 of Surah Al-Hijr, where various proofs were explained and questions in this regard were answered).

Question

It might be said that "Batil" (falsehood), as mentioned, means "the opposite of truth," whereas you and other commentators have interpreted it as "Mubtil" (that which invalidates) or the act of invalidating.

Answer

The answer to this question can be understood by noting a subtle point: the Quran does not say that no falsehood will exist after this Heavenly Book; rather, it says that no falsehood will come to this Heavenly Book (note the pronoun in the phrase "ya'tihi"). The meaning of this statement is that nothing can approach it to invalidate it (reflect on this).

Ayahs

مَّا يُقَالُ لَكَ إِلَّا مَا قَدْ قِيلَ لِلرُّسُلِ مِن قَبْلِكَ ۚ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَذُو مَغْفِرَةٍ وَذُو عِقَابٍ أَلِيمٍ (٤٣)
Nothing is said to you except what has already been said to the apostles before you. Indeed your Lord is One who forgives and also metes out a painful retribution. (43)
وَلَوْ جَعَلْنَـٰهُ قُرْءَانًا أَعْجَمِيًّا لَّقَالُوا۟ لَوْلَا فُصِّلَتْ ءَايَـٰتُهُۥٓ ۖ ءَا۬عْجَمِىٌّ وَعَرَبِىٌّ ۗ قُلْ هُوَ لِلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ هُدًى وَشِفَآءٌ ۖ وَٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ فِىٓ ءَاذَانِهِمْ وَقْرٌ وَهُوَ عَلَيْهِمْ عَمًى ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يُنَادَوْنَ مِن مَّكَانٍۭ بَعِيدٍ (٤٤)
Had We made it a non-Arabic Quran, they would have said, ‘Why have not its signs been articulated?’ ‘What! A non-Arabian [scripture] and an Arabian [prophet]!?’ Say, ‘It is guidance and healing for those who have faith. As for those who are faithless, there is a deafness in their ears and it is lost to their sight: [to them it is as if] they were called from a distant place.’ (44)
وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَا مُوسَى ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ فَٱخْتُلِفَ فِيهِ ۗ وَلَوْلَا كَلِمَةٌ سَبَقَتْ مِن رَّبِّكَ لَقُضِىَ بَيْنَهُمْ ۚ وَإِنَّهُمْ لَفِى شَكٍّ مِّنْهُ مُرِيبٍ (٤٥)
Certainly We gave Moses the Book, but differences arose about it; and were it not for a prior decree of your Lord, judgement would have been made between them, for they are in grave doubt concerning it. (45)
مَّنْ عَمِلَ صَـٰلِحًا فَلِنَفْسِهِۦ ۖ وَمَنْ أَسَآءَ فَعَلَيْهَا ۗ وَمَا رَبُّكَ بِظَلَّـٰمٍ لِّلْعَبِيدِ (٤٦)
Whoever acts righteously, it is for [the benefit of] his own soul, and whoever does evil, it is to its detriment, and your Lord is not tyrannical to His servants. (46)

Commentary

The Quran is Guidance and Healing

Since the disbelievers of Mecca had launched the severest struggle against the religion of Islam and the person of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), and the preceding verses reported their deviation regarding the proofs of Monotheism and their disbelief and denial of Divine verses, the first verse under discussion, as a consolation to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and to teach a lesson of steadfastness and perseverance to all Muslims who fall under the pressure of enemies, states: "The unjust attributions made against you are the same as those made to the messengers before you" (مَا يُقَالُ لَكَ إِلَّا مَا قَدْ قِيلَ لِلرُّسُلِ مِنْ قَبْلِكَ).

If they call you possessed or a sorcerer, they gave these same attributions to the great Prophets of the past; and if they call you a liar, those Prophets were not immune to such labels either.

In short, neither is your call toward the religion of Monotheism and Truth a new matter, nor is their slander and denial. Stand firm, pay no heed to these words, continue the call to Monotheism, and know that Allah is with you.

Some commentators have suggested that the meaning of this sentence is that the words spoken to you by Allah are the same words spoken to previous Prophets [1], but considering the subsequent sentence and the following verses, the first interpretation seems more correct.

Then, at the end of the verse, it adds: "Indeed, your Lord is the Possessor of forgiveness and also the Possessor of painful punishment" (إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَذُو مَغْفِرَةٍ وَ ذُو عِقابٍ أَلِيمٍ).

Mercy and forgiveness are for those who accept, and a painful punishment is for those who deny, slander, and rise in opposition.

This sentence is, in reality, a glad tiding and encouragement for the believers, and a warning and threat for the disbelievers.

Placing "forgiveness" before "punishment," as in other instances, is evidence of "mercy preceding wrath," as stated in the supplication: "O He whose mercy preceded His wrath" [2].

In the next verse, the discourse turns to the excuses of these stubborn individuals and addresses one of these strange pretexts, which was their saying: Why was the Quran not revealed in a non-Arabic (Ajam) tongue so that we might grant it greater importance and so that non-Arabs might also benefit from it? Apparently, their goal was that the masses of people would not understand anything from it, eliminating the need for them to say: "Do not listen to this Quran and make noise therein" (لَا تَسْمَعُوا لِهَذَا الْقُرْآنِ وَالْغَوْا فِيهِ) (as mentioned in previous verses) [1]. Here, the Quran says in response to them: "Had We made it a non-Arabic Quran, they would surely have said: 'Why are its verses not explained in detail? Why is it complex? We cannot make sense of it!'" (وَلَوْ جَعَلْنَاهُ قُرْآنًا أَعْجَمِيًّا لَقَالُوا لَوْلَا فُصِّلَتْ آيَاتُهُ).

Then they would add: "Is it truly strange—a non-Arabic Quran from an Arabic Prophet?" (ءَأَعْجَمِيٌّ وَعَرَبِيٌّ).

Or they would say: "A non-Arabic book for an Arabic nation?"

And now that it has been revealed in the Arabic language, and everyone understands its concepts well and reaches the depth of the Quran's message and call, they still cry out: "Do not listen to this Quran, and prevent people from hearing it with clamor and vain talk."

In short, they are the diseased of heart; whatever plan is devised and whatever program is implemented, they find a fault with it and fabricate an excuse. If it is Arabic, they call it sorcery and incantation; if it is non-Arabic, they deem it incomprehensible; and if it were a mixture of Arabic and non-Arabic words, they would label it discordant! [2]

[^1]: This interpretation is mentioned in "Majma' al-Bayan" and "Tafsir al-Kabir" by Fakhr al-Razi as a possibility, but they themselves preferred the first interpretation. [^2]: Dua al-Jawshen al-Kabir, Section 19, Sentence 8.

It should be noted that "A'jami" (non-Arabic/foreign) comes from the root "Ujmah," meaning a lack of eloquence and ambiguity in speech. Non-Arabs are called "Ajam" because their language is not well understood [by Arabs], and "A'jam" refers to someone who does not express matters well (whether they be Arab or non-Arab).

Therefore, the word "A'jami" is the same as "A'jam" combined with the relative suffix "ya."

Then the Quran, addressing the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), adds: "Say: This Heavenly Book is a guidance and a healing for those who believe" (قُلْ هُوَ لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا هُدًى وَشِفَاءٌ).

"But as for those who do not believe, there is a heaviness in their ears" and they do not comprehend it (وَالَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ فِي آذَانِهِمْ وَقْرٌ).

"And it is blindness upon them" (وَهُوَ عَلَيْهِمْ عَمًى) [1]. They are exactly like those who are called from a far-off place (أُولَئِكَ يُنَادَوْنَ مِنْ مَكَانٍ بَعِيدٍ).

And it is clear that such people neither hear nor see! Indeed, to find the path and reach the destination, the existence of light alone is not enough; a seeing eye is also necessary. Likewise, for instruction, the existence of a learned and eloquent preacher is not sufficient; there must also be a listening ear.

There is no doubt in the delicacy of raindrops and their life-giving effect, but "in the garden, greenery grows, and in the salt-marsh, weeds."

Those who approached the Quran with a spirit of seeking the truth found guidance and healing in it; their moral and spiritual diseases were treated in the infirmary of the Quran. Then they packed their bags and moved swiftly toward the Abode of the Beloved in the light of the Quran's guidance.

But the prejudiced stubborn ones, the enemies of truth and reality, and those who had already decided to oppose the Prophets—what benefit could they derive from it?

They were like the blind and the deaf situated at a distant point; they suffered from a double deafness and blindness—both in terms of the tools of sight and hearing and in terms of spatial distance! Some commentators have noted that linguists say of one who understands: "You hear from nearby," and of one who does not understand: "You are called from afar" (where even if you hear a murmur, you do not grasp the meaning of the words) [1]. Regarding how the Quran is a source of healing and treatment for the agonizing pains of humanity, we have provided a detailed discussion under verse 82 of Surah al-Isra (Volume 12, page 236 onwards).

In the next verse, to console the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and the early believers, it says: Do not be distressed by the denial, stubbornness, and excuse-making of these headstrong people; this has a long history. "We gave Moses the Heavenly Book, and it was differed upon; some accepted it and some turned to denial" (وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنَا مُوسَى الْكِتَابَ فَاخْتُلِفَ فِيهِ).

And if you observe that We do not hasten in punishing these stubborn enemies, it is because educational interests require that they be free, and that the proof be completed as much as possible. "And had a word not preceded from your Lord in this regard, it would have been judged between them," and Divine punishment would have swiftly overtaken them (وَلَوْلَا كَلِمَةٌ سَبَقَتْ مِنْ رَبِّكَ لَقُضِيَ بَيْنَهُمْ).

This Divine decree was based on the interests of guiding humanity and completing the proof, and this Sunnah (tradition) has been applied among all past nations and is applied regarding your nation as well.

But they still have not believed this reality "and they are in doubt about your Quran, a doubt mixed with suspicion and pessimism" (وَإِنَّهُمْ لَفِي شَكٍّ مِنْهُ مُرِيبٍ).

"Murib" comes from the root "Rayb," meaning a doubt mixed with pessimism, suspicion, and anxiety. Therefore, the meaning of the sentence is that the polytheists not only doubt your words but also claim that there are contrary indications within them that cause pessimism! Some commentators have suggested that the latter sentence refers to the Jews and the Book of Moses—meaning that this people still doubt the Torah—but this meaning seems unlikely, and the first interpretation is the apparent one [1].

[^1]: Some commentators interpreted the above sentence to mean "the Quran becomes a cause of blindness for this group," whereas according to Raghib in "Al-Mufradat" and Ibn Manzur in "Lisan al-Arab," the phrase "Amaya 'alayhi" means "it became so confused for him that he became like the blind in relation to it." Therefore, the correct meaning of the sentence is what we have provided in the text. [^1]: "Tafsir al-Qurtubi" under the verses discussed. [^1]: Note that this verse appeared with exactly this wording in Surah Hud, verse 110.

In the last verse under discussion, a general law that the Quran has emphasized many times is stated in connection with human actions, which serves as a completion to the previous discussion regarding the believers' benefit from the Quran and the deprivation of the faithless from this source of Divine grace. It states: "Whoever does a righteous deed, its benefit is for himself, and whoever does evil, he has done evil to himself, and your Lord is never unjust to His servants" (مَنْ عَمِلَ صَالِحًا فَلِنَفْسِهِ وَمَنْ أَسَاءَ فَعَلَيْهَا وَمَا رَبُّكَ بِظَلَّامٍ لِلْعَبِيدِ).

Therefore, if they do not believe in this Book and this great religion, they harm neither Allah nor you, for all good and evil return to their owners, and it is they who harvest the sweet or bitter fruit of their own deeds.

Key Points

1. Free Will and Justice

The phrase "And your Lord is never unjust to His servants" (وَمَا رَبُّكَ بِظَلَّامٍ لِلْعَبِيدِ) is clear evidence for the issue of free will and freedom of choice. It expresses the reality that Allah neither punishes anyone without cause nor increases anyone's punishment without reason. His program is pure justice, for the source of injustice is deficiency, lack, ignorance, or carnal desire, and His Holy Essence is sanctified from all such things.

The use of "Zallam," which is an intensive form meaning "one who is very unjust," here and in some other Quranic verses, may indicate that even a slight punishment without cause from the Great God would be an instance of "great injustice," as such is never expected of Him.

Some have also said that because He has many servants, if He were to commit even a small injustice against each one, He would be "Zallam" (this and the previous interpretation are not contradictory).

In any case, the Quran, in these clear verses, strikes a line of cancellation through the school of determinism (Jabr), which is a source of spreading corruption, endorsing various uglinesses, and negating any commitment or responsibility. It holds everyone responsible for their actions and considers the results of each person's deeds to primarily affect themselves.

Thus, in a narration from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida (عليه السلام), we read that one of his companions asked: "Does Allah compel His servants to commit sins?"

He replied: "No, rather He gives them choice and grants them respite so that they may repent."

He asked again: "Does He charge His servants with what they cannot bear?"

The Imam (عليه السلام) said: "How could He do that when He Himself says: 'And your Lord is never unjust to His servants'?"

Then the Imam (عليه السلام) added: "My father Musa ibn Ja'far (عليه السلام) narrated from his father Ja'far ibn Muhammad (عليه السلام): 'Whoever claims that Allah compels His servants to commit sins or charges them with what they cannot bear, do not eat from the animal he slaughters, do not accept his testimony, do not pray behind him, and do not give him anything from the Zakat!'" (In short, do not apply the rulings of Islam to him) [1]. The above narration also subtly points out that the school of determinism leads to "charging with what cannot be borne," for if a person is compelled to sin on one hand and forbidden from it on the other, it is a clear instance of charging someone with the impossible.

[^1]: 'Uyun Akhbar al-Rida, as quoted in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 555.

2. Sin and the Deprivation of Blessings

The Commander of the Faithful, Ali (عليه السلام), says: "By Allah! No people were ever in the embrace of the comforts of life and then had them removed except due to the sins they committed, for Allah is never unjust to His servants!" Then he added: "If people, when calamities descend upon them and blessings are removed from them, would turn to their Lord with sincerity of intention and hearts full of love for Allah, asking Him to solve their problems, Allah would return to them everything that had fled and would rectify for them everything that had become corrupt" [1]. From this statement, the relationship between sins and the deprivation of blessings becomes clearly evident.

3. Why So Many Excuses?

Undoubtedly, the Arabic language is among the richest and most profound languages in the world, yet the greatness of the Quran is not because it is in Arabic. Rather, its being in Arabic is because Allah sends every Prophet in the language of his own people, so that they may believe first, and then the scope of his religion may expand to others.

However, the excuse-makers, who like children raised a new illogical point every day, showed through their childish and contradictory words that they were not seeking the truth. One day they would say: Why was this Quran revealed only in the Arabic tongue? Would it not have been better if all or part of it were in another language so that others might benefit? (While they had another goal: they wanted the Arab masses to be deprived of its extraordinary attraction).

And if this request of theirs had been fulfilled, they would have said: How can he be an Arab and his book be non-Arabic?

In short, every day they occupied themselves and others with an excuse to turn away from the path of truth.

Fundamentally, "excuse-making" is always evidence that a person has another pain they do not wish to disclose. The pain of this group was that the masses of people were intensely attracted to the Quran, and thus their interests were severely endangered. To extinguish the light of Islam, they resorted to every means.

End of Part (Juz') 24 of the Holy Quran.

Beginning of Part (Juz') 25 of the Holy Quran, Verse 47 of Surah Fussilat.

[^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 188.

Ayahs

۞ إِلَيْهِ يُرَدُّ عِلْمُ ٱلسَّاعَةِ ۚ وَمَا تَخْرُجُ مِن ثَمَرَٰتٍ مِّنْ أَكْمَامِهَا وَمَا تَحْمِلُ مِنْ أُنثَىٰ وَلَا تَضَعُ إِلَّا بِعِلْمِهِۦ ۚ وَيَوْمَ يُنَادِيهِمْ أَيْنَ شُرَكَآءِى قَالُوٓا۟ ءَاذَنَّـٰكَ مَا مِنَّا مِن شَهِيدٍ (٤٧)
On Him devolves the knowledge of the Hour, and no fruit emerges from its covering and no female conceives or delivers except with His knowledge. On the day when He will call out to them, ‘Where are My ‘partners’?’ They will say, ‘We informed You that there is no witness amongst us.’ (47)
وَضَلَّ عَنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَدْعُونَ مِن قَبْلُ ۖ وَظَنُّوا۟ مَا لَهُم مِّن مَّحِيصٍ (٤٨)
What they used to invoke before has forsaken them, and they know there is no escape for them. (48)

Commentary

The Secrets of All Things Are With Him

In the final verse of the previous discussion, the talk was about the return of good and evil deeds to their owners, which contained an implicit reference to the issue of reward and punishment on the Day of Resurrection.

Here, a question arises for the polytheists: when will this Resurrection you speak of arrive?! In the verses under discussion, the Quran first responds to this question by saying: Knowledge of the timing of the Resurrection is exclusive to Allah "To Him alone is referred the knowledge of the Hour" (إِلَيْهِ يُرَدُّ عِلْمُ السَّاعَةِ).

No messenger prophet nor any archangel is aware of it, and they must not be aware, so that everyone may consider its occurrence possible at any moment, and the specific educational effect of this expectation remains preserved in all those who are accountable.

Then it adds: Not only is the knowledge of the timing of the Resurrection exclusive to Allah, but the knowledge of the secrets of this world and its hidden and manifest beings also belongs to Him: "No fruit comes out of its sheath, nor does any female—human or animal—conceive or give birth, except with His knowledge" (وَ ما تَخْرُجُ مِنْ ثَمَراتٍ مِنْ أَكْمامِها وَ ما تَحْمِلُ مِنْ أُنْثى‌ وَ لا تَضَعُ إِلَّا بِعِلْمِهِ‌) [^1]. Neither in the world of plants nor in the world of animals and humans is a seed formed, fertilized, or born except by the command of the Great Allah and according to His knowledge and wisdom.

"Akmam" is the plural of "Kumm" (rhyming with jinn), meaning the sheath or husk that covers the fruit. "Kumm" (rhyming with qum) means the sleeve that covers the hand, and "Kummah" (rhyming with qubbah) means the skullcap placed on the head [^1]. Tabarsi says in Majma' al-Bayan: "Takammama al-rajulu fi thawbihi" is said when a person wraps himself in his garment.

Fakhr Razi, in his commentary, interprets "Akmam" as the skin or shell in which the fruit is located.

Some commentators have also interpreted it as "Wi'a al-Thamara" (the vessel of the fruit) [^2]. It appears that all these interpretations return to a single meaning. Since one of the most delicate and precise matters in the world of living beings is the issue of fertilization in the womb and birth, the Quran emphasizes it specifically, whether in the world of animals or plants.

Yes, He is the One who knows which seed is formed in which womb and at what time, and when it is born; which fruit is fertilized and when it splits its sheath and shell to emerge.

Then it adds: This group that denies the Resurrection or takes it in mockery, "On the day when the Resurrection is established, He will call out to them: 'Where are the partners you used to attribute to Me?' They will say: 'O Lord! We declare to You that none of us is a witness to those claims'" (وَ يَوْمَ يُنادِيهِمْ أَيْنَ شُرَكائِي قالُوا آذَنَّاكَ ما مِنَّا مِنْ شَهِيدٍ) ^3 [^4].

[^1]: The "min" in "min thamarat" and "min untha," as well as in "min shahid" which follows in the verse, is extra (za'idah) for emphasis. [^1]: Raghib in Al-Mufradat. [^2]: Tafsir al-Mizan and Tafsir al-Maraghi.

[^4]: Another possibility has been suggested in the interpretation of this sentence: that among us today, no one bears witness to the existence of a partner for You, and everyone denies this matter.

What we used to say were baseless and unfounded words—words originating from ignorance, unawareness, or blind imitation. Today, better than any other time, we understand how void and baseless those claims were.

And in this state, they see no trace of the deities they used to call upon before, "and all they used to invoke before will have vanished from them" (وَ ضَلَّ عَنْهُمْ ما كانُوا يَدْعُونَ مِنْ قَبْلُ‌).

In fact, the scene of the Resurrection is so terrifying for them that even the memory of the idols vanishes from their minds—those same deities before whom they once prostrated, for whom they sacrificed, and for whose sake they sometimes gave their lives, imagining them to be their refuge in times of misery and the solvers of their problems; all vanish like mirages.

Yes, "on that day they will realize that there is no place of refuge or escape for them" (وَ ظَنُّوا ما لَهُمْ مِنْ مَحِيصٍ‌).

"Mahis" is from the root "hays" (rhyming with hayf), meaning to turn back, deviate, or withdraw from something. Since "mahis" is a noun of place, this word means a place of escape or a refuge [^1]. "Zannu" from the root "zann" has a broad meaning in the language: sometimes it means certainty (yaqin) and sometimes it means suspicion or assumption. In the verse under discussion, it means certainty, because on that day they become certain that they have no way of escape or salvation from the divine punishment.

"Raghib" says in Al-Mufradat: "Zann" refers to a belief that arises from evidence and clues; this belief sometimes becomes strong and reaches the stage of certainty, and sometimes it is weak and does not exceed the level of assumption.

[^1]: In Volume 10 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 325, this word was mentioned as being from the root "mahas," which should be corrected.

Ayahs

لَّا يَسْـَٔمُ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ مِن دُعَآءِ ٱلْخَيْرِ وَإِن مَّسَّهُ ٱلشَّرُّ فَيَـُٔوسٌ قَنُوطٌ (٤٩)
Man is never wearied of supplicating for good, and should any ill befall him, he becomes hopeless and despondent. (49)
وَلَئِنْ أَذَقْنَـٰهُ رَحْمَةً مِّنَّا مِنۢ بَعْدِ ضَرَّآءَ مَسَّتْهُ لَيَقُولَنَّ هَـٰذَا لِى وَمَآ أَظُنُّ ٱلسَّاعَةَ قَآئِمَةً وَلَئِن رُّجِعْتُ إِلَىٰ رَبِّىٓ إِنَّ لِى عِندَهُۥ لَلْحُسْنَىٰ ۚ فَلَنُنَبِّئَنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بِمَا عَمِلُوا۟ وَلَنُذِيقَنَّهُم مِّنْ عَذَابٍ غَلِيظٍ (٥٠)
If, after distress has befallen him, We let him have a taste of Our mercy, he says, ‘This is my due! I do not think the Hour will ever come, and in case I am returned to my Lord, I will indeed have the best [reward] with Him.’ So We will surely inform the faithless about what they have done, and will surely make them taste a harsh punishment. (50)
وَإِذَآ أَنْعَمْنَا عَلَى ٱلْإِنسَـٰنِ أَعْرَضَ وَنَـَٔا بِجَانِبِهِۦ وَإِذَا مَسَّهُ ٱلشَّرُّ فَذُو دُعَآءٍ عَرِيضٍ (٥١)
When We bless man, he is disregardful and turns aside; but when an ill befalls him, he makes protracted supplications. (51)
قُلْ أَرَءَيْتُمْ إِن كَانَ مِنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ ثُمَّ كَفَرْتُم بِهِۦ مَنْ أَضَلُّ مِمَّنْ هُوَ فِى شِقَاقٍۭ بَعِيدٍ (٥٢)
Say, ‘Tell me, if it is from Allah and you disbelieve in it, who will be more astray than those who are in extreme defiance.’ (52)

Commentary

These Small-Minded Humans

In connection with the discussion in the previous verses regarding the polytheists and their fate, the verses under discussion provide a portrayal of the state of these weak and faithless individuals—a vivid and enlightening depiction, and a living, clear manifestation of these short-sighted and small-minded people.

First, It says: "Man never tires of praying for good (wealth, property, and the blessings of life)" (لا يَسْأَمُ الْإِنْسانُ مِنْ دُعاءِ الْخَيْرِ).

The furnace of his greed never cools down; the more he finds, the more he wants, and no matter how much he is given, he is never satiated.

"But if the world turns its back on him, his blessings vanish, and evil, hardship, and poverty afflict him, he becomes utterly despondent and hopeless" (وَ إِنْ مَسَّهُ الشَّرُّ فَيَؤُسٌ قَنُوطٌ).

The "man" referred to here is the "unrefined human" whose heart has not been illuminated by the light of Divine knowledge, faith in the Lord, and a sense of responsibility for the Day of Recompense. These are individuals who, due to flawed worldviews, are trapped within the confines of the material world and lack a lofty spirit to see beyond it or perceive sublime human values.

Indeed, when the world smiles upon them, they are joyful and arrogant, and when it turns away, they are grieved and hopeless. They have neither a sanctuary to grant them refuge nor a bright lamp to sprinkle the light of hope upon their hearts.

Furthermore, it should be noted that "du'a" (prayer/supplication) sometimes means calling upon someone and sometimes means requesting something; in the verse under discussion, it carries the second meaning. Therefore, "لا يَسْأَمُ الْإِنْسانُ مِنْ دُعاءِ الْخَيْرِ" means that man never tires of seeking and requesting good things.

Regarding whether "ya'us" and "qanut" carry the same meaning—i.e., a hopeless person—or two different meanings, and what the difference between them is, there is discussion among the commentators:

Some consider both to have the same meaning (for emphasis) [^1]. However, others consider "ya'us" (from the root ya's) to mean the presence of hopelessness within the heart, and "qanut" to mean manifesting it on the face and in action [^2]. The late Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan distinguished between the two by stating that ya's is hopelessness regarding "good" (khayr), while qanut is hopelessness regarding "mercy" (rahmah) [^3].

[^1]: Al-Mizan, under the verses discussed (Vol. 17, p. 426). [^2]: Fakhr Razi in Tafsir al-Kabir (Vol. 27, p. 137) and Tafsir Ruh al-Ma'ani (Vol. 25, p. 4). [^3]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 9, p. 18.

However, what is gathered from the usage of the terms ya's and qanut in the Holy Quran is that they are used in nearly the same sense. For example, in the story of Joseph, we read that Jacob warned his sons against ya's (despair) from Allah's mercy, while they were both internally hopeless regarding finding Joseph and had manifested signs of despair (Yusuf: 87).

Regarding qanut, in the story of the glad tidings of a son to Abraham, we read that he expressed surprise at the matter, but the angels said to him: "بَشَّرْناكَ بِالْحَقِّ فَلا تَكُنْ مِنَ الْقانِطِينَ" (We have given you glad tidings in truth, so be not of the despondent!) (Al-Hijr: 55).

In the next verse, He points to another undesirable state of humans distanced from knowledge and faith—namely, the state of arrogance and self-satisfaction—saying: "And if We let him taste mercy from Us after an adversity has touched him, he says, 'This is due to me (my own merit), and I deserved such a status and blessing'" (وَ لَئِنْ أَذَقْناهُ رَحْمَةً مِنَّا مِنْ بَعْدِ ضَرَّاءَ مَسَّتْهُ لَيَقُولَنَّ هذا لِي) [^1]. This arrogant wretch forgets that if it were not for Allah's grace, he would be afflicted with calamity instead of this blessing. He is like the arrogant Qarun, who, when Allah granted him great wealth as a test and he was told to do good as Allah had done good to him, said: "No, whatever I have is because of the knowledge and inherent merit I possess!" (قالَ إِنَّما أُوتِيتُهُ عَلى‌ عِلْمٍ عِنْدِي) (Al-Qasas: 78).

[^1]: Some commentators have said that the phrase "هذا لِي" means "this blessing will always remain for me," which implies its permanence. However, the interpretation mentioned above is more appropriate, though there is no contradiction between the two and they can be combined: he considers himself deserving of the blessing and also imagines it to be permanent and eternal.

The verse continues: This arrogance eventually leads him to deny the Hereafter, and he says: "I do not think the Hour (Resurrection) will ever occur" (وَ ما أَظُنُّ السَّاعَةَ قائِمَةً).

"And even if there were a Resurrection, if I am returned to my Lord, surely there will be for me with Him the best rewards and many blessings!" The God who has honored me so in this world will surely provide an even better reception in the Hereafter! (وَ لَئِنْ رُجِعْتُ إِلى‌ رَبِّي إِنَّ لِي عِنْدَهُ لَلْحُسْنى‌).

A similar point appears in Surah Al-Kahf, in the story of the two friends, one of whom was wealthy and chose the path of disbelief and arrogance, while the other remained steadfast in faith. There, the Quran quotes the arrogant man who owned gardens and flowing springs: "ما أَظُنُّ أَنْ تَبِيدَ هذِهِ أَبَداً - وَ ما أَظُنُّ السَّاعَةَ قائِمَةً وَ لَئِنْ رُدِدْتُ إِلى‌ رَبِّي لَأَجِدَنَّ خَيْراً مِنْها مُنْقَلَباً" (I do not think this will ever perish, and I do not think the Hour will occur. And if I am returned to my Lord, I will surely find a better place of return than this!!) (Al-Kahf: 35-36).

But Allah threatens these arrogant and headstrong individuals at the end of this verse: "We will surely inform the disbelievers of what they did, and We will surely make them taste a severe punishment" (فَلَنُنَبِّئَنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بِما عَمِلُوا وَ لَنُذِيقَنَّهُمْ مِنْ عَذابٍ غَلِيظٍ) [^1]. This same meaning appears elsewhere in the Holy Quran with a different expression: "And if We let him taste favor after adversity has touched him, he says, 'The evils have gone away from me.' Indeed, he is exultant and boastful" (Hud: 10).

[^1]: "Severe punishment" ('adhabun ghaliz) means "intense and dense punishment."

In the next verse, He recounts the third state that occurs for such humans during the arrival and departure of the material world: the state of forgetfulness during blessing and lamentation during calamity.

He says: "When We bestow favor upon man, he turns away and distances himself with arrogance" (وَ إِذا أَنْعَمْنا عَلَى الْإِنْسانِ أَعْرَضَ وَ نَأى‌ بِجانِبِهِ‌).

"But when evil touches him, he is full of lengthy supplication (to have it removed)" (وَ إِذا مَسَّهُ الشَّرُّ فَذُو دُعاءٍ عَرِيضٍ‌).

"Na'a" (نأى) is from the root na'y, meaning to distance oneself. When "janib" (side/flank) follows it, it is an idiom for arrogance and pride, because arrogant people turn their faces away and distance themselves with indifference.

"Arid" (عريض) means wide as opposed to long, and the Arabs use these two expressions to denote abundance and great quantity.

A similar meaning appears in Surah Yunus: "And when affliction touches man, he calls upon Us, whether lying on his side or sitting or standing; but when We remove his affliction from him, he passes on as if he had never called upon Us for an affliction that touched him. Thus is made fair-seeming to the transgressors that which they used to do" (Yunus: 12).

Indeed, such is the human devoid of faith and piety, who is constantly trapped in these states: "greedy," "arrogant," and "forgetful" when blessings arrive, and "despondent," "hopeless," and "full of lamentation" when blessings depart.

In contrast, the men of truth and true followers of the prophets' school are so capacious and substantial that neither the arrival of blessings changes them, nor does the departure of the world make them weak, powerless, or hopeless. They are the embodiment of: "Men whom neither commerce nor sale distracts from the remembrance of Allah" (رِجالٌ لا تُلْهِيهِمْ تِجارَةٌ وَ لا بَيْعٌ عَنْ ذِكْرِ اللَّهِ). The most profitable trades and the most beneficial incomes do not make them heedless of Allah's remembrance. They well understand the philosophy behind the bitterness and sweetness of life; they know that sometimes bitterness is a wake-up call, and sweetness is a Divine trial and test.

Sometimes bitterness is the punishment for heedlessness, and blessings are meant to stir the servants' sense of gratitude.

It is noteworthy that in the above verses, the terms "adhaqna" (We let taste) and "massahu" (touched him) are used, implying that with even a slight turn of the world or a minor loss of blessings, the state of these shallow individuals changes, and they take the path of arrogance or hopelessness and despair. They are so short-sighted and weak that, according to the famous proverb, they turn sour with one unripe grape and sweet with one raisin.

Indeed, one of the most important effects of faith in Allah is that very breadth of soul, loftiness of vision, expansion of the chest (sharh al-sadr), and readiness to face problems and calamities and to combat the undesirable excitements of blessings.

Commander of the Faithful Ali عليه السلام, in the course of supplications in which he provides a model for his companions, says:

"We ask Allah, the Glorified, to make us and you among those whom no blessing makes arrogant, whom no goal falls short of the obedience of their Lord, and whom no regret or sorrow afflicts after death" (Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 64).

In the final verse under discussion, He addresses these stubborn individuals with a final word, explaining the well-known rational principle of "averting potential harm" with a clear statement. Addressing the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, He says: "Say, 'Have you considered: if this [Quran] is from Allah (the One and Only) and you disbelieve in it, who is more astray than one who is in extreme opposition and far-reaching deviation?'" (قُلْ أَ رَأَيْتُمْ إِنْ كانَ مِنْ عِنْدِ اللَّهِ ثُمَّ كَفَرْتُمْ بِهِ مَنْ أَضَلُّ مِمَّنْ هُوَ فِي شِقاقٍ بَعِيدٍ) [^1]. Of course, this discourse is for those upon whom no logical argument works; it is effectively the final word spoken to stubborn, arrogant, and prejudiced persons, which is: If you do not 100% accept the truth of the Quran, monotheism, and the existence of the world after death, you certainly have no proof to negate them either. Therefore, the possibility remains that the Quran's call and the issue of the Resurrection are realities. Then, think about what a dark and terrifying fate you will have with this deviation, intense opposition, and stance against this Divine school.

This is the same argument that the Imams of the religion علیهم السلام would raise before stubborn individuals in the final stage. As we read in a hadith in the book al-Kafi:

Imam Sadiq عليه السلام had many discussions with "Ibn Abi al-Awja," the materialist and atheist of his time. In the final stage when he met him during the Hajj pilgrimage, some of the Imam's companions said, "It seems Ibn Abi al-Awja has become a Muslim!" The Imam said: "He is more blind-hearted than that; he will never become a Muslim." When his eyes fell upon Imam Sadiq عليه السلام, he said, "O my master and chief!" The Imam asked:

"What brought you to this place?"

He replied: "The habit of the body, the custom of the land, and to observe the madness, the truth, and the stone-throwing that the people are engaged in!" The Imam said:

"You are still upon your rebellion and deviation, O Abdul Karim!" [^1]

He wanted to start speaking, but the Imam said: "There is no dispute during Hajj" (لا جِدالَ فِي الْحَجِ‌) and pulled his cloak from his hand, saying this sentence:

"If the matter is as you say—and it is not as you say—we are saved and you are saved. But if the matter is as we say—and it is as we say—we are saved and you are destroyed!"

"Ibn Abi al-Awja" turned to his companions and said: "I felt a stinging pain in my heart, take me back." They took him back, and he died shortly after! [^2]

[^1]: "A-ra'aytum" is usually interpreted as "Tell me" (akhbiruni), and we had a detailed discussion on this in Vol. 5, p. 231. [^1]: "Abdul Karim" was the original name of "Ibn Abi al-Awja," and since he denied God, the Imam عليه السلام specifically called him by this name to make him feel ashamed. [^2]: Al-Kafi, Vol. 1, p. 61 (Book of Tawhid, Chapter on the Origination of the World).

Key Point

A question arises here: In the verses under discussion, we read: "When evil and distress touch man, he is full of lengthy and continuous supplication" (إِذا مَسَّهُ الشَّرُّ فَذُو دُعاءٍ عَرِيضٍ‌).

However, in verse 83 of Surah Al-Isra, it says: "And when evil touches him, he is despondent" (و اذا مسه الشركان يؤسا). (The same theme appeared in the verses under discussion as well).

The question is: Continuous and abundant supplication is a sign of hope, whereas the other verse says he becomes hopeless.

Some commentators, in response to this question, have divided people into two groups: a group that becomes completely hopeless during hardships, and a group that persists in supplication and lamentation [^1]. Others have said: The "hopelessness" refers to despair regarding ordinary means, and this does not contradict requesting from Allah and praying [^2]. It is also possible that "full of lengthy supplication" does not mean requesting from Allah, but rather abundant lamentation and crying out. Evidence for this is verse 20 of Surah Al-Ma'arij, which says: "Indeed, man was created anxious: when evil touches him, he is fretful" (إِنَّ الْإِنْسانَ خُلِقَ هَلُوعاً إِذا مَسَّهُ الشَّرُّ جَزُوعاً).

[^1]: Tafsir Ruh al-Bayan, Vol. 8, p. 280. [^2]: Tafsir al-Mizan, Vol. 17, p. 428. However, this interpretation does not seem very appropriate considering that the above verses are in censure of such persons, whereas cutting hope from apparent means and turning to Allah is not only not a flaw but is worthy of praise and commendation.

Alternatively, these two states occur in two stages for these small-minded individuals: initially, they begin to pray and make many requests from elders and prophets, and they raise cries and lamentations. But it is not long before the state of despair permeates their entire being, and they become hopeless and silent.

Ayahs

سَنُرِيهِمْ ءَايَـٰتِنَا فِى ٱلْـَٔافَاقِ وَفِىٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ حَتَّىٰ يَتَبَيَّنَ لَهُمْ أَنَّهُ ٱلْحَقُّ ۗ أَوَلَمْ يَكْفِ بِرَبِّكَ أَنَّهُۥ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ شَهِيدٌ (٥٣)
Soon We shall show them Our signs in the horizons and in their own souls until it becomes clear to them that He is the Real. Is it not sufficient that your Lord is witness to all things? (53)
أَلَآ إِنَّهُمْ فِى مِرْيَةٍ مِّن لِّقَآءِ رَبِّهِمْ ۗ أَلَآ إِنَّهُۥ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ مُّحِيطٌۢ (٥٤)
Indeed, they are in doubt about the encounter with their Lord! Indeed, He embraces all things! (54)

Commentary

Signs of Truth in the Macrocosm and Microcosm

In these two verses that conclude Surah Fussilat, reference is made to two important matters which, in reality, serve as a summary of the discussions in this Surah. The first verse speaks of Monotheism (Tawhid) or the Quran, and the second verse speaks of the Resurrection (Ma'ad).

In the first verse, He says: "We will soon show them Our signs in the horizons and within themselves, until it becomes clear to them that He is the Truth" (سَنُرِيهِمْ آياتِنا فِي الْآفاقِ وَ فِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ حَتَّى يَتَبَيَّنَ لَهُمْ أَنَّهُ الْحَقُ‌).

"Signs in the horizons" (Ayat al-Afaq) include the creation of the sun, moon, and stars with the precise system governing them, and the creation of various animals, plants, mountains, and seas with their countless wonders and marvels, and the diverse mysterious creatures whose secrets of creation are discovered anew at every moment—each being a sign and evidence of the truth of His Pure Essence.

And "signs within themselves" (Ayat al-Anfus) include the creation of the various systems of the human body: the system governing the astonishing structure of the brain, the rhythmic movements of the heart, the vessels, tissues, and bones, the fertilization of the ovum and the development of the fetus in the mother's womb, and even higher than that, the secrets and wonders of the human soul, every corner of which is a book of knowledge regarding the Lord and Creator of the universe.

It is true that these signs have already been sufficiently presented by the Lord, but considering the phrase "We will show them" (سَنُرِيهِمْ), which is in the "present/future tense" (mudaari') and indicates continuity, this presentation continues perpetually. Even if a human were to live for hundreds of thousands of years, they would encounter a new discovery and a fresh presentation of Divine signs at every moment, for the secrets of this universe are inexhaustible! All books of natural sciences and anthropology in all their dimensions (anatomy, physiology, psychology, psychoanalysis) and sciences related to botany, zoology, organic matter, astronomy, and others, are in reality all books of Monotheism and the knowledge of Allah (Ma'rifatullah), because they generally unveil astonishing secrets that express the infinite knowledge, wisdom, and power of the original Creator of this world.

Sometimes one of these sciences—rather, one branch out of dozens of branches of one of these sciences—occupies the entire life of a scientist, and at the end, he says: "Alas, I still know nothing of this field, and what I have learned so far has only guided me to the depth of my ignorance!" At the end of this verse, He completes this subtle and interesting statement with another beautiful and meaningful phrase, adding: "Is it not sufficient for them that your Lord is a Witness over all things?" (أَ وَ لَمْ يَكْفِ بِرَبِّكَ أَنَّهُ عَلى‌ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ شَهِيدٌ) [^1]. What testimony is greater and higher than this: that He has written His power with the pen of creation upon the forehead of all beings? On the surface of tree leaves, within the petals of flowers, amidst the mysterious layers of the brain, upon the delicate membranes of the eye, on the canvas of the sky, and in the heart of the earth—in short, He has written the signs of His Monotheism upon everything and has given a creative testimony.

What was mentioned above is one of the two well-known and famous interpretations of this verse, according to which the entire discourse of the verse revolves around the issue of Monotheism and the manifestation of the signs of Truth in the horizons and the souls.

However, the other interpretation pertains to the miraculous nature of the Quran. Its summary is that Allah says in this verse: "We will show them Our various miracles and signs in different parts of the Arabian Peninsula and other regions of the world, and regarding these polytheists themselves, so they may know that this Quran is the Truth."

The "signs in the horizons" include the victory of Islam on various battlefields and in the arena of logical debate, and subsequently the various regions that the religion of Islam conquered, where it came to govern the thoughts of the people. The same population in Mecca who, at the time of the revelation of these verses, were seemingly such a minority that they had no capacity for any positive activity—yes, those very people emigrated by the command of the Lord, and in a short time, everything came under their banner, and their school of thought was welcomed by a vast group of people across the world.

And the "signs within themselves" were the victory of the Muslims over the polytheists of Mecca in the Battle of Badr and on the day of the Conquest of Mecca, and the penetration of the light of Islam into the hearts of many of them.

These signs in the horizons and within themselves showed that the Glorious Quran is the Truth. The same God who is a Witness over all things also testified to the truth of the Quran through this means.

Each of these two interpretations has its supporting evidence and preferences, but considering the end of the verse and the following verse, the first interpretation seems closer [^1]. There are other opinions regarding the interpretation of this verse, but since they were not significant, we have refrained from mentioning them.

The final verse of this Surah expresses the primary source of the misfortunes of this polytheistic, corrupt, and oppressive group, saying: "Unquestionably, they are in doubt about the meeting with their Lord" (أَلا إِنَّهُمْ فِي مِرْيَةٍ مِنْ لِقاءِ رَبِّهِمْ‌).

And because they do not believe in the Reckoning and Reward, they commit every crime and submit to every shameful act. Veils of negligence and pride have fallen over their hearts, and forgetting the meeting with their Lord has dragged them down from the peak of human greatness to a state of fall.

But they must know that "Unquestionably, He is encompassing of all things" (أَلا إِنَّهُ بِكُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ مُحِيطٌ). All their deeds, words, and intentions are clear before His knowledge, and all of it will be recorded and preserved for the great court of the Resurrection.

"Miryah" (pronounced like jizyah or qaryah) means hesitation in decision-making, and some consider it to mean a great doubt or suspicion. The original root of this word is from "maraytu al-naqah," meaning to squeeze a camel's udder after milking in the hope that some remains might be in it; since this act is done with doubt and uncertainty, the word has come to mean "doubt and hesitation."

And if "argumentation" is called "miraa'," it is also because a person tries to extract what is in the mind of the opponent.

In reality, the final sentence is a response to some of the doubts of the disbelievers regarding the Resurrection, including: "How is it possible for these scattered and mixed soils to be separated from one another? And who can gather the parts of every human being? Furthermore, who is aware of the intentions, deeds, and words of all humans throughout human history?"

In response to all these questions, the Quran says: The God who encompasses all things—all these matters are clear to Him. The proof of His encompassing knowledge over everything is His governance (tadbir) over all things; how is it possible for the Governor of the world to be unaware of the state of the world?!

Some commentators have also considered this verse to be related to the issue of Monotheism, not the Resurrection, saying: The meaning is that these arguments regarding the Monotheism of the Lord do not benefit this stubborn group of disbelievers, for they deny the clearest proof of Monotheism—namely, the presence of Allah everywhere and His witnessing over everything. In such a state, how can they benefit from the proofs of Monotheism? [^1] However, considering that the expression "meeting with Allah" (Liqa' Allah) in the Glorious Quran is usually a metaphor for the Resurrection, this interpretation seems unlikely.

Key Points

1- The Argument from "Order" and the Argument of the "Truthful" (Siddiqin)

We know that among the proofs for Monotheism, philosophers give great importance to two arguments: First, the argument from "Order" (Nizam), and then the argument of the "Truthful" (Siddiqin).

The argument from order, as its name suggests, leads from the system of the universe and its secrets and subtleties to the Source of knowledge and power that created and governed it. The Glorious Quran is full of reasoning based on this clear proof, and everywhere it presents examples of the signs of Truth in the heavens and the earth, the world of life, and various creatures, opening a clear path toward His Pure Essence through them.

This proof is understandable for all strata of society, and everyone can benefit from it according to the extent of their understanding and knowledge—the greatest scientists according to their understanding, and those with little or no education according to their perception.

But the argument of the "Truthful" (Siddiqin) is a proof by which they reach the "Essence" through the "Essence" and realize the Necessary Being through His own existence. In other words, here, contingent beings and creatures are not the intermediaries for proving His existence; rather, His Essence itself becomes the proof for His Pure Essence, becoming an instance of "O He who pointed to His Essence by His Essence" [^1], or an instance of "Allah witnesses that there is no deity except Him" [^2]. This reasoning is a complex philosophical argument that only those aware of its principles can grasp in its depth. Our intention here is not to elaborate on it, as its place is in philosophical books; rather, the intention is only to state the fact that some commentators have considered the beginning of the verse "We will show them Our signs in the horizons..." as a reference to the argument from order and cause and effect, and its end "Is it not sufficient for them that your Lord is a Witness over all things?" as a reference to the argument of the Truthful. However, there is no clear evidence within the verse itself for this claim.

2- The Reality of Allah's Encompassing of All Things

One must never imagine that Allah's encompassing of all beings is, for example, similar to the way the air surrounding the Earth encompasses the Earth, for such encompassing would imply limitation. Rather, the meaning of the Lord's encompassing of all things is a much more precise and subtle concept: it is the dependence of all beings in their very essence upon His Holy Existence.

In other words, in the world of existence, there is only one original and self-subsisting existence, and the rest of the contingent beings are all reliant and dependent upon Him; if this connection were removed for a single moment, all would perish and become non-existent.

This encompassing is the same reality expressed in the profound words of Amir al-Mu'minin Ali عليه السلام in the first sermon of Nahj al-Balagha with this phrase: "He is with everything, but not by way of proximity, and He is different from everything, but not by way of separation."

And this is perhaps what Imam Husayn عليه السلام expressed in that content-rich, brilliant, and eloquent Supplication of 'Arafah: "Can anything other than You have a manifestation that You do not have, such that it could be the one to manifest You? When were You ever hidden that You should need a proof to point to You? And when were You ever distant that the effects should be what lead to You? Blind is the eye that does not see You as a Watcher over it! And lost is the deal of a servant for whom You have not appointed a share of Your love." [^1]

When did You ever leave the heart that I should seek You? When did You ever become hidden that I should make You manifest? You came forth with a hundred thousand manifestations so that I Might behold You with a hundred thousand eyes.

3- Signs in the "Horizons" and "Within Themselves"

Whatever we may be able to deny, we cannot deny the existence of an astonishing, calculated system in the universe, around us, and within ourselves. Sometimes a scientist spends his entire life studying the structure and secrets of the eye, the brain, or the heart, and reads many books written about each of these, yet still confesses that there are many unopened secrets regarding these subjects.

Furthermore, it should not be forgotten that the knowledge of today's scientists is the product of the accumulated studies of millions of scientists throughout human history.

Thus, everywhere and in whatever we look at, we see the effects of infinite knowledge and power behind them. Every plant that grows from the earth says, "He is One, He has no partner," and if we split the heart of every atom, we see a sun within it.

It is not bad to close our eyes to the important and complex subjects of the world and turn to simple and so-called mundane subjects to see that they too are clear proofs for proving the existence of that Great Source.

It is useful to provide two examples here:

1- You surely know that in the sole of every human foot, there is a specific arch that does not seem important at all. However, when we hear that in special military examinations, young men who rarely lack this arch of the foot are exempted from service or assigned to clerical work, we realize what a sensitive role this very simple matter plays in human existence—that without it, one tires quickly when standing and lacks the necessary ability for military service when walking. It is here that we confess everything in the world is calculated, even the arch of the foot.

2- Inside the human eye and mouth, there are bubbling springs that are precisely regulated, and from very delicate and narrow openings, two completely different fluids flow throughout life without interruption. If they were not there, man would neither have the power to see nor the ability to speak, chew, or swallow food. In other words, without these two seemingly small matters, life would be impossible for a human.

If the surface of the eye were not constantly moist, the rotation of the eyeball would be painfully irritating and impossible, and the contact of the eyelids with the surface of the eye would scratch it, or rather, stop it from moving! If the tongue, throat, and mouth were not moist, neither speaking nor swallowing food would be possible. You have experienced how difficult it becomes for a person to speak, or even breathe and eat, when the mouth and throat become slightly dry—let alone if this water were cut off entirely.

The inside of the nose must also be constantly moist so that the continuous contact and passage of air can occur easily.

Interestingly, from a narrow opening that is considered the drainage of the eye, the water that bubbles from the tear glands flows down into the nose and keeps it moist. If one day this very delicate and narrow opening were blocked—as we see in some patients—a flood of tears would constantly flow down the face, creating an unsightly appearance and a bothersome condition. And if the proportion of the tear springs to the capacity of these openings were disrupted, the same issue would repeat.

Regarding the salivary glands of the mouth, the issue is the same: a deficiency makes the tongue, mouth, and throat dry, and an excess interferes with speaking and causes water to flow out from the mouth.

The composition of eye water, which has a salty taste, is such that it perfectly preserves the delicate tissues of the eye, and when dust or other objects settle on the eye, it automatically gushes out and ejects the intrusive object.

However, the composition of saliva has no other taste, so that the taste of food is preserved, and it contains minerals that are a very effective factor for digesting food.

If we reflect on the physical and chemical aspects of these two bubbling springs and their precise, calculated system, and their subtleties, benefits, and blessings, we will be certain that a blind and deaf factor of chance could not have brought them into existence. Studying just this one seemingly small sign within ourselves is enough to clarify for us that He is the Truth: "We will show them Our signs in the horizons and within themselves, until it becomes clear to them that He is the Truth."

Imam Sadiq عليه السلام, in the famous Hadith of Tawhid al-Mufaddal, which is full of mentions of the Lord's signs in the horizons and within themselves, says in a short and meaningful reference to this matter: "O Mufaddal! Reflect on the saliva and the benefit it contains, for it has been made to flow constantly into the mouth to moisten the throat and the uvula so they do not dry out. For if these places were left as such, it would lead to the destruction of man. Furthermore, you would not be able to swallow food if there were no moisture in the mouth to facilitate it; observation testifies to this." [^1]

Moving beyond the human body, his soul is a center of wonders that has bewildered all scientists, and there are thousands upon thousands of these clear signs in the universe, all testifying that "He is the Truth."

It is here that we involuntarily join our voice with the Master of Martyrs, Imam Husayn عليه السلام, saying: "Blind is the eye that does not see You!" O Allah! Blind be the eye that does not see You!

End of Surah Fussilat 12/ Rabi' al-Awwal/ 1405 15/ 9/ 1363 (Solar Hijri)
